Next — Lesson 06: GitOps + Platform Engineering + SRE + Multi-Tenancy. If you've decided Kubernetes is the right fit, the next question is how mature teams operate it.

Suvis Guru · K-COM · Lesson 5 of 17 · grounded in kubernetes.io · CNCF case studies
Lesson 06· GitOps · Platform Eng · SRE · Multi-Tenancy

📍 Today's stop in K-Town: Public Library.

📍 Public Library · lesson 6 of 16Lesson 6 of 16, Public Library.

Lesson 06 · how mature teams operate Kubernetes

GitOps · Platform Engineering · SRE · Multi-Tenancy

Once you have Kubernetes, the question becomes: how do we run it day-to-day? Five practices that mature organizations converge on. Think: a well-run library.

🚨 The 3 AM Nightmare

Your one ops engineer is the bottleneck for every deploy across 6 product teams. Every change is a ticket. Tickets pile up. Deploys take days. The ops engineer is on call for everything they didn't write. They're burning out and the company can't ship. This lesson is about the org shape that fixes this — and why it's not just a tooling change.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: Five practices that compound — GitOps + platform engineering + SRE + service ownership + multi-tenancy. None is a Kubernetes feature; all are practices Kubernetes enables.

Section 1 · Concept

Five practices, one operating model

Once you have Kubernetes installed, the next question is bigger than the technology itself: how do we run it as a team, day after day, without it becoming a mess? Mature organizations converge on five interlocking practices.

GitOps moves the source of truth to a Git repository. An automated agent in the cluster watches Git and reconciles. Same loop pattern as a Kubernetes controller, just with Git as the desired state. Tools: Argo CD, Flux CD.

Platform engineering is the discipline of building an internal platform — a "paved road" — that product teams consume. The platform team owns Kubernetes, observability, secrets, CI/CD, ingress; product teams ship apps onto it via a thin interface.

SRE (Site Reliability Engineering) is Google's operating model from the 2016 SRE Book. Defines reliability with SLIs (indicators), SLOs (objectives), SLAs (agreements); spends an error budget on velocity until reliability drops.

Service ownership ("you build it, you run it") puts the team that wrote the service on its on-call rotation. Aligns incentives toward reliability without bureaucratic enforcement.

Multi-tenancy is sharing one cluster (or platform) across multiple teams or customers. Soft = trust between tenants, isolation by namespace + RBAC + quotas. Hard = no trust, separate clusters or virtual clusters.

These five compound. None is a Kubernetes feature; all are practices Kubernetes enables.

Section 1.5 · The 5 practices, broken down

Each practice, one tile

Five tiles. Each shows what the practice is and the core idea in one sentence.

📚

1 · GitOps

Git is the source of truth

Every change starts as a Git commit. An agent (Argo CD, Flux) pulls from Git and reconciles the cluster. Drift = automatically reverted.

🛣️

2 · Platform engineering

build the paved road

A platform team builds the cluster, observability, secrets, ingress as a paved road. Product teams just ship apps onto it.

📊

3 · SRE

measure, then trade off

Measure reliability with SLIs/SLOs. Spend the error budget on shipping velocity. When budget runs out, slow down and fix.

📞

4 · Service ownership

you build it, you run it

The team that built the service is on-call for it. The people who can fix it are the people who feel it. Reliability follows from incentive.

🏢

5 · Multi-tenancy

share with rules

Multiple teams share one cluster. Soft tenancy = namespaces + RBAC + quotas. Hard tenancy (untrusted) = separate clusters or vCluster.

All five compound: platform team builds the road, product teams own their services, GitOps drives changes, SRE measures reliability, multi-tenancy keeps everyone politely sharing.

⏸ Pause and check

GitOps puts the source of truth in:

Answer: c. An agent in the cluster watches Git and reconciles. Same loop pattern as a Kubernetes controller, just one level up.

Section 2 · Before & After

Ops as a ticket queue vs platform + ownership

The shift is not just technical — it's how the org is structured. Before: one ops team is the bottleneck. After: a platform team enables many product teams.

Same number of services, different org shape. The bottleneck moves from "one ops queue" to "many teams doing what they need."
Before · ops queue

Operations is a separate ticket queue. To deploy, a developer files a ticket; the ops team patches a server, runs a script, closes the ticket. When the system breaks at 3 AM, ops gets paged — the developer doesn't.

Knowledge of how things actually run lives only in ops. Developers ship code blind. Ops burns out. Velocity falls.

After · platform + ownership

The platform team builds and runs the cluster + observability + secrets + CI/CD. Product teams own their services end-to-end: write the YAML, commit to Git, agent reconciles, they're on the rotation when their service pages.

SRE practices measure reliability. Service ownership aligns incentives. Multi-tenancy keeps teams isolated. The org scales without the ops queue scaling.

What didn't change: people still need to be on call. Software still breaks. The 3 AM page still happens. What changes is who answers it (the team that owns the code) and how it gets fixed (with the same tools and observability the team uses every day).

Section 3 · Analogy

The well-run library

Imagine a public library. Every change to the shelves starts with a card written in the catalog. A librarian reads new cards and walks to the shelf to make the change. Nobody walks past the librarian and rearranges books — and if they do, the librarian notices the drift and puts the books back the way the catalog says.

Different sections (Fiction, Reference, Periodicals) are tended by their own staff who know that section deeply. The head librarian watches statistics — books misshelved per day, late returns, complaints — and decides how much risk the library can absorb this quarter. Schools, scholars, and the public all share the same building, separated by reading rooms, by hours, by privileges.

Kubernetes orgs converge on this exact shape. The Git catalog, the controller-librarian, the section-owning teams, the head-librarian SRE, the multi-tenant building. Every piece of the metaphor maps cleanly to a real practice.

In the story……in Kubernetes
The card catalogGit (the source of truth for every change)
The librarianThe GitOps controller (Argo CD / Flux)
The library building and reading roomsThe platform (cluster + tooling) the platform team builds
The section-owning staffProduct teams owning their services and on-call
The head librarianSRE practice (SLIs / SLOs / error budgets)
Schools, scholars, public sharing the buildingMulti-tenancy (many teams sharing one cluster)

⚠️ The analogy stops here: the GitOps controller doesn't argue with you. If the catalog says "shelve it under N", it shelves it under N — even if a human walked by and put the book somewhere "more sensible."

Section 4 · Two-level explanation

Two ways to hear it

Same five practices, two depths.

ELI5 · explain like I'm 5

Think of a really good library. Every book change starts with a written card in the catalog. A librarian reads the cards and moves the books. Different sections have their own helpers who know their books well. The head librarian watches how things are going. And lots of different people use the library at the same time without bumping into each other. Kubernetes is run by teams that work like this library.

ELI10 · explain like I'm 10

Five practices that compound. GitOps: Git is the source of truth, an agent in the cluster reconciles the cluster to match Git — same loop pattern as a Kubernetes controller, just at a higher level. Platform engineering: a platform team builds the paved road (cluster, observability, secrets, CI/CD); product teams ship apps onto it. SRE: measure reliability with SLIs/SLOs, spend an error budget on shipping velocity. Service ownership: the team that built it is on-call for it. Multi-tenancy: namespaces + RBAC + quotas for trusted tenants; separate clusters or vCluster for untrusted. None of these is a Kubernetes feature; they're all practices Kubernetes enables.

⏸ Pause and check

"You build it, you run it" means:

Answer: b. The team that wrote the code is paged when it breaks. Reliability emerges from incentive — they fix the things that wake them up.

Section 5 · Real-world scenarios

Where these practices show up

Four moments where one or more of the practices made a real difference.

Retail company adopts Argo CD across 20 product teams

Pre-GitOps, average production change took 2 days from commit to live and required three approvals. Post-GitOps (Argo CD), the same change took 14 minutes via PR merge to the env repo. The platform team published a paved-road template — Helm chart skeleton + Argo Application + ServiceMonitor + AlertmanagerConfig — and any new service got all of those for free. Two years later they introduced soft multi-tenancy: each team got its own namespace, ResourceQuotas, NetworkPolicies, and an Argo Project scoped to its repos.

SaaS company introduces error budgets — the team finally shipped less

A 30-engineer SaaS team kept missing their 99.9% availability target. They formally adopted SRE practices: defined SLIs (request success rate), set SLOs (99.9% over 28 days), tracked error budget burn. When they hit 50% of budget burned in week 1, they froze new feature rollouts and spent the week fixing the unstable database connection pool. Within 6 months, they were comfortably hitting their SLO and shipping faster on average — because they no longer needed to fix the same outages over and over.

Fintech moves to "you build it, you run it"

A 50-engineer fintech moved from a centralized ops team to per-team on-call. Initial reaction: developer protest. ("We didn't sign up to be on-call!") Six months later: bug fix turnaround was 70% faster (because the team that owned the code was paged). On-call load was actually lower than expected (because product teams started writing better tests, better runbooks, better health checks — they were the ones being woken up). Reliability emerged from incentive, not from process.

University cluster shared across 12 research labs

A research computing platform serves 12 different research labs. Different labs have different trust levels (some run code from external collaborators), so the platform uses a mix: trusted labs get namespaces with RBAC + quotas (soft tenancy); untrusted labs get a vCluster (hard tenancy, separate API server, isolated). Both models share the same physical cluster underneath, dramatically reducing cost compared to one cluster per lab.

Section 6 · Animation

Watch GitOps reconcile (and revert drift)

Three modes. The first shows normal GitOps flow. The second shows what happens when someone bypasses Git. The third shows a new service onboarding via the paved road.

Watching: normal GitOps flow. Git declares 3 replicas; cluster has 3 Pods. Controller watches both, acts only when there's a gap.
Section 7 · Misconceptions, flashcards & quiz

Lock it in

Three common misconceptions to clear up first, then the flashcards and quiz.

Common Misconceptions

Myth: GitOps is just "use Git for your YAML."
Truth: It's an agent in the cluster continuously reconciling state to match Git. Manual edits get reverted. That's the part that makes it powerful.
Myth: SRE = "ops, but renamed."
Truth: SRE adds explicit reliability targets (SLOs) and an error budget — when you've spent the budget on outages, you slow down feature work and fix things. It's an operating model, not a job title.
Myth: Multi-tenancy means one cluster per team.
Truth: Soft tenancy = one cluster, isolated by namespaces + RBAC + quotas (for trusted tenants). Hard tenancy (separate clusters or vCluster) is for untrusted tenants only.
What is GitOps?
tap to flip
An operating model where Git is the source of truth and an agent in the cluster continuously reconciles. Tools: Argo CD, Flux. Coined by Weaveworks (Alexis Richardson, 2017).
What are the 4 GitOps principles?
tap to flip
Declarative; versioned and immutable in Git; pulled automatically by an agent; continuously reconciled. Per opengitops.dev (CNCF GitOps Working Group).
What is platform engineering?
tap to flip
The discipline of building an internal platform — a "paved road" — that product teams consume. The platform team owns the cluster, observability, secrets, CI/CD; product teams ship apps on it.
SRE in one sentence?
tap to flip
Site Reliability Engineering. Google's operating model. Defines reliability with SLIs/SLOs/error budgets, treats infrastructure as a software problem, automates ops as code.
SLI · SLO · error budget?
tap to flip
SLI = a measured indicator (request success rate). SLO = the target (99.9%). Error budget = how much unreliability you can spend (0.1% = 43 min/month). When budget runs out, slow down and fix.
What is service ownership?
tap to flip
"You build it, you run it." The team that wrote the service is on its on-call rotation. Aligns incentives toward reliability without bureaucratic enforcement.
Soft vs hard multi-tenancy?
tap to flip
Soft: trusted tenants share one cluster, isolation via namespaces + RBAC + ResourceQuotas + NetworkPolicy. Hard: untrusted tenants need separate clusters or virtual clusters (vCluster), strict admission policy, dedicated nodes.
What is drift in GitOps?
tap to flip
When the live cluster diverges from the Git source of truth (someone hand-edited a resource, expired cert, manual hot-fix). The GitOps controller detects drift and either alerts or reverts to Git.
⏸ pause the animation

A developer SSHes into a production node and changes a ConfigMap by hand. The cluster is GitOps-managed by Argo CD. Within minutes, the change disappears. Why?

answerArgo CD continuously reconciles the live cluster state to the Git source of truth. The hand-edited ConfigMap is drift; Argo CD detects it and reverts the resource to whatever Git says. The right path is to commit the change to Git (and let Argo CD apply it), or to mark the resource as ignored if it's intentionally externally managed.

Why does "you build it, you run it" make services more reliable over time?

answerIncentive alignment. The team that wrote the code feels the on-call pages. They can't externalize the cost of bugs onto a separate ops team. Over weeks, they invest in better tests, better observability, better deploy patterns — because they will be paged otherwise. Reliability emerges from incentive, not from process.
🎬 Choose Your Own Adventure

Your platform team runs an "ops ticket queue" for 6 product teams. Tickets average 2 days to close. Click to see the year-end review. ▼

year-end reviewTwo engineers quit (burnout). The remaining ops engineer is on call for 6 teams' code they didn't write. Deploys average 2 days from PR to live. Product velocity is half of what it could be. The fix isn't more tooling — it's restructuring: platform team owns the road; product teams own their services and their pages. Reliability follows incentive.
Words from this lesson · open if you want a quick reference
GitOps
Operating model where Git is the source of truth and an agent reconciles the cluster. Tools: Argo CD, Flux. opengitops.dev for the formal four principles.
Argo CD / Flux
The two main GitOps controllers. Argo CD has a UI; Flux is more CLI/Kubernetes-native. Both pull from Git and reconcile.
Platform engineering
Building an internal platform (paved road) for product teams. Owns cluster, observability, secrets, CI/CD, ingress.
SRE
Site Reliability Engineering. Google's operating model. SLIs/SLOs/error budgets, infrastructure-as-code.
SLI · SLO · SLA
Indicator (measurement) · Objective (target) · Agreement (with consequences). The SRE vocabulary.
Error budget
How much unreliability you can spend before missing your SLO. 99.9% SLO = 0.1% budget = 43 min/month.
Service ownership
"You build it, you run it." Same team writes code, ships it, and is on-call for it.
Multi-tenancy (soft vs hard)
Soft: namespaces + RBAC + quotas (trusted teams). Hard: separate clusters or vClusters (untrusted tenants).

🎯 If you remember nothing else: Five practices that compound — GitOps + platform engineering + SRE + service ownership + multi-tenancy. None is a Kubernetes feature; all are practices Kubernetes enables.

✓ Lesson 06 complete

Five practices that compound: GitOps + platform engineering + SRE + service ownership + multi-tenancy. Together, they're how mature orgs operate Kubernetes.

Next — Lesson 07: Kubernetes History · CNCF · Releases · KEPs · Feature Gates. Where Kubernetes came from, how it ships, and why "alpha → beta → GA" matters.

Suvis Guru · K-COM · Lesson 6 of 17 · grounded in opengitops.dev · Google SRE Book · CNCF
Lesson 07· History · CNCF · Releases · KEPs · Feature Gates

📍 Today's stop in K-Town: K-Town Rail Yard.

📍 K-Town Rail Yard · lesson 7 of 16Lesson 7 of 16, K-Town Rail Yard.

Lesson 07 · how Kubernetes evolves

History · CNCF · Releases · KEPs · Feature Gates

Where Kubernetes came from, who governs it, how often it ships, and why every new feature graduates through alpha → beta → GA like new train signals tested first on a quiet branch line.

🚨 The 3 AM Nightmare

You enabled a Kubernetes alpha feature in production "to get ahead." Six months later you upgrade the cluster. The feature was renamed. Your manifests don't apply. Your deploys are broken. You spend two on-call weeks rebuilding workloads. This lesson is about the lifecycle every Kubernetes feature graduates through — and why "GA only in prod" is the rule that keeps you out of this hole.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: Every Kubernetes feature graduates alpha → beta → GA, like train signals tested on a quiet branch line first. Production runs on GA.

Section 1 · Concept

Where Kubernetes came from, and how it ships

Kubernetes didn't appear out of nowhere. It descended from systems Google had been running for over a decade, and it's developed today by hundreds of contributors at hundreds of companies through a formal process. This lesson covers five foundational topics about how Kubernetes evolves as a project.

Lineage: Borg → Omega → Kubernetes. Borg was Google's internal cluster manager from the early 2000s; the Borg paper was published in 2015. Omega was a research successor (paper 2013). Kubernetes was open-sourced in June 2014 and reached 1.0 in July 2015. The name is Greek for "helmsman"; "K8s" is K + 8 letters + s.

CNCF: The same month Kubernetes hit 1.0 (July 2015), it was donated to the Cloud Native Computing Foundation — a Linux Foundation project hosting vendor-neutral cloud-native open-source projects. Kubernetes became the CNCF's first graduated project in March 2018. The CNCF now hosts hundreds of projects (Prometheus, Envoy, Helm, Argo, etc.) — see the CNCF landscape.

Release cadence: Kubernetes ships roughly 3 minor releases per year (it was 4/year through 2022, slowed to 3/year in 2023). Each minor release is patched for ~14 months — 12 months of active patches plus a 2-month EOL transition. So at any moment, the most recent 3 minor versions are actively patched.

KEPs: Kubernetes Enhancement Proposals — the formal change-management process. Any non-trivial change goes through a KEP: a markdown document in kubernetes/enhancements, reviewed by the relevant SIG (Special Interest Group), tracked through stages.

Feature gates: Per-feature on/off flags. Each feature progresses through three stages: Alpha (off by default, may break or be removed), Beta (since 1.24+, new beta features are off by default; API may still change), GA / Stable (on by default, backward-compatibility guaranteed, removal needs multi-release deprecation).

[ deep dive — skip if new ]Conformance: The CNCF Certified Kubernetes program. Distributions that pass the test suite (run via hydrophone, historically sonobuoy) can call themselves "Certified Kubernetes." Vendor-specific features sit outside conformance — they're product additions, not the standard.

Section 1.5 · The 5 topics, broken down

Each topic, one tile

The five things to remember about how Kubernetes evolves as a project.

📜

1 · Lineage

Borg → Omega → Kubernetes

Open-sourced June 2014 · 1.0 in July 2015. Built by ex-Borg engineers at Google. "K8s" = K + 8 letters + s.

🏛️

2 · CNCF

the home of cloud-native

Linux Foundation · 2015. Hosts hundreds of cloud-native projects. K8s was its first graduated project (March 2018).

📅

3 · Releases

~3 minors per year

3 minors per year (since 2023; was 4). Each patched ~14 months. Last 3 minors actively supported.

📝

4 · KEPs

enhancement proposals

A KEP is a markdown doc proposing a change. SIGs (Special Interest Groups) review and approve. Tracks every non-trivial K8s change.

🚦

5 · Feature gates

alpha · beta · GA

Per-feature on/off flags. Alpha: off, may break. Beta: stabilizing, may change. GA: on, stable, backward-compatible.

All five describe how the project moves: history shaped it · CNCF homes it · cadence ships it · KEPs propose changes · feature gates graduate them.

Section 1.7 · The three stages, in detail

Alpha · Beta · GA — what each one means for you

The three stages map cleanly to "should I use this in production?" Read across.

Alpha

α · branch line

  • Off by default. Must be explicitly enabled.
  • May break. API can change incompatibly.
  • May vanish. Removed without deprecation.
  • Use only in dev/test clusters. Never production.
Beta

β · moderately busy

  • Off by default for new betas (since K8s 1.24).
  • Mostly stable. API may still change shape.
  • Has deprecation cycle if removed.
  • Vet case-by-case for prod. Read the KEP.
GA · Stable

GA · main intercity

  • On by default. Cannot easily be disabled.
  • Backward compatibility guaranteed.
  • Removal needs multi-release deprecation.
  • The default choice for production workloads.

Production rule: use GA features only. Vet beta case-by-case. Reserve alpha for dev/test clusters.

⏸ Pause and check

What does "GA" mean in Kubernetes?

Answer: b. GA carries the strongest compatibility guarantee K8s offers.

Section 2 · Before & After

Pre-Kubernetes orchestration vs the de facto standard

Kubernetes wasn't the first attempt at running containers at scale. There were several competitors. Then Kubernetes won.

2014: six competing orchestrators. 2026: Kubernetes is the de facto standard, available as a managed service on every cloud.
Before · 2014

Running containers at scale meant either home-grown shell scripts or one of several competing systems: Borg (Google internal), Mesos + Marathon, Docker Swarm, Nomad, CoreOS fleet. Each had its own API. Each had its own community. Workload portability across clouds was a custom integration job per cloud.

After · today

Kubernetes is the standard. Every major cloud provides a managed Kubernetes service (EKS, AKS, GKE) with the same upstream API. Workload portability is roughly real — same manifests work across clouds. The CNCF landscape has hundreds of projects, but Kubernetes is the spine.

What didn't get easier: Kubernetes is not simple. Its API surface is enormous, conformance only covers the API not the operational reality, and distributions still vary in defaults, networking, storage, and security.

Section 3 · Analogy

Train-line testing for new signals

When a railway introduces a new signal system, they don't switch the entire intercity network at once. They test on a quiet branch line first, where breakage is acceptable and few passengers are affected. Once the signal works there, they roll it out to a moderately busy line — more traffic, more learning. Once that's proven, they finally enable it on the main intercity routes where millions of passengers depend on it.

By the time you ride the main line, the signals are boring on purpose. They've been observed long enough to be predictable. The reason intercity travel feels safe is precisely because the signals were tested somewhere quiet first.

Kubernetes feature gates work exactly the same way. Alpha is the branch line: things break, that's the point. Beta is the moderately busy line: most things work, but the railroad tells you "we may still tweak the signal pattern." GA is the main intercity line: stable, predictable, and removing a signal requires its own multi-year process.

In the story……in Kubernetes
Quiet branch lineAlpha (off by default, may break, dev / test only)
Moderately busy lineBeta (mostly works, may still tweak — vet for prod case by case)
Main intercity lineGA / Stable (boring, predictable, default for production)
The railroad's signal-testing processKubernetes Enhancement Proposals (KEPs)
The signal designersSIGs (Special Interest Groups)
The railroad's operating standards bodyThe CNCF and the Kubernetes project itself

⚠️ The analogy stops here: trains test new signals once per signal. Kubernetes tests every feature, separately, every release. There's no shared "main line approval."

Section 4 · Two-level explanation

Two ways to hear it

Same five topics, two depths.

ELI5 · explain like I'm 5

Kubernetes is a tool that lots of people work on together. When they want to add something new, they try it carefully in a tiny test version (alpha), then in a slightly bigger version (beta), and only then put it in the real version everyone uses (GA). This is like testing a new train signal on a quiet train line before using it on the busy ones.

ELI10 · explain like I'm 10

Kubernetes started inside Google as a project called Borg, then a research version called Omega, then the open-source Kubernetes (2014). It's now run by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF), and developed by hundreds of contributors. New features go through a formal process called a KEP (Kubernetes Enhancement Proposal) and graduate through three stages — alpha (off, unstable), beta (off-by-default for new features, mostly stable), GA (on-by-default, backward compatible). Three minor releases per year, each supported for ~14 months. The CNCF runs a "Certified Kubernetes" conformance program so distros (EKS, AKS, GKE, OpenShift) all behave the same at the API level.

⏸ Pause and check

Your team is debating whether to enable a new beta feature in production.

Answer: b. Beta APIs can still change shape. The right move is vetting per feature, per cluster, per risk profile — not a blanket yes or no.

Section 5 · Real-world scenarios

Where these decisions show up

Four moments where lineage, releases, KEPs, or feature gates shaped a real outcome.

Platform team's "GA only in prod" rule

A platform team standardised on "we ship workloads against GA APIs only; beta features are evaluated case by case; alpha is dev-only." This rule meant they were never burned by Gateway API's beta-period field renames (v1beta1 → v1) — they waited an extra year and got the GA contract. Their next-door neighbour team made the opposite call (run alpha aggressively for early features) and spent two on-call weeks rebuilding workloads when an alpha API was renamed in a minor release.

"Kubernetes-compatible" vs "Certified Kubernetes"

A team picked a cheap distribution that called itself "Kubernetes-compatible." Six months in, they hit a feature that worked on EKS but not on this distribution — turns out the distribution had skipped some less-common API endpoints. Manifests had to be rewritten. They migrated to a Certified Kubernetes distribution and the issue went away. Lesson: "Certified Kubernetes" is a real CNCF program with a real test suite (run via hydrophone). "Compatible" is a marketing term.

Tracking KEPs to plan a year ahead

A senior platform engineer at a large enterprise reads every KEP that gets accepted. He keeps a roadmap of "features that will be GA in K8s 1.30," "features still in alpha but worth watching," and "features being deprecated." This lets the platform team know — months in advance — what'll change and what'll be removed, so workloads can adapt before the upgrade. The KEP repo (github.com/kubernetes/enhancements) is his most-read GitHub project.

Quarterly K8s upgrade cadence

An ops team upgrades production clusters every 4 months — one minor version at a time. They never skip versions (Kubernetes officially supports skip-1 upgrades for the control plane but most teams find one-at-a-time safest). They also never run the latest version in prod — they wait one minor release for "early adopter pain" to be reported and patched. So: latest minor in dev/staging, minus-one in production. Once the support window ends, they upgrade.

Section 6 · Animation

Watch a feature graduate alpha → beta → GA

Three modes. Each shows the same feature at a different stage of its lifecycle, with the adoption pattern that comes with it.

Watching: a feature in alpha. Off by default. May break or vanish. ~10% of users have it enabled (early adopters in dev clusters).
Section 7 · Misconceptions, flashcards & quiz

Lock it in

Three common misconceptions to clear up first, then the flashcards and quiz.

Common Misconceptions

Myth: Alpha = "almost done."
Truth: Alpha = "off by default, may break, may vanish without deprecation." Production-hostile by design.
Myth: Kubernetes belongs to Google.
Truth: It came out of Google in 2014, but it's been governed by the CNCF (a Linux Foundation project) since 2015. Hundreds of contributors at hundreds of companies.
Myth: "Kubernetes-compatible" and "Certified Kubernetes" mean the same thing.
Truth: Certified Kubernetes is a CNCF program with a specific test suite. "Compatible" is marketing.
What is the Kubernetes lineage?
tap to flip
Borg (Google internal since early 2000s, paper 2015) → Omega (Google research, paper 2013) → Kubernetes (open-sourced June 2014, 1.0 in July 2015, CNCF first graduated project March 2018). "K8s" = K + 8 letters + s.
What is the CNCF?
tap to flip
Cloud Native Computing Foundation. Linux Foundation project formed 2015. Hosts vendor-neutral cloud-native open-source projects. K8s was its first graduated project (March 2018). landscape.cncf.io shows hundreds of projects.
Current K8s release cadence?
tap to flip
Roughly 3 minor releases per year (since 2023; was 4/year before). Each minor release is patched for ~14 months (12 months active + ~2 months EOL transition). The most recent 3 minors are supported at any time.
What is a KEP?
tap to flip
Kubernetes Enhancement Proposal — a markdown document in github.com/kubernetes/enhancements reviewed by the relevant SIG (Special Interest Group). Any non-trivial change goes through a KEP.
What is a feature gate?
tap to flip
A runtime flag that turns an individual feature on or off. Default depends on stage. Per-component flag (--feature-gates). Cluster-wide on managed services.
What does 'alpha' mean?
tap to flip
Alpha = off by default, may change incompatibly or be removed without deprecation, may be buggy. Production rule: use only in dev/test clusters. Reserve for evaluation, not real workloads.
What does 'GA' mean?
tap to flip
General Availability. Stable. On by default. Backward-compatible behaviour. Multi-release deprecation cycle if removal ever happens. The default choice for production.
What is conformance?
tap to flip
CNCF Certified Kubernetes program. Distributions that pass the test suite (run via hydrophone, historically sonobuoy) can call themselves Certified Kubernetes. Vendor-specific features sit OUTSIDE conformance — they're product additions.

Why is "we use a feature only after it goes GA" usually the right operational rule?

answerBecause GA carries the strongest compatibility guarantee Kubernetes offers — backward-compatible behaviour, multi-release deprecation if it's ever removed, on by default. Beta APIs can still change shape; alpha can vanish. Operating production on GA features means upgrades won't surprise you; operating on beta or alpha means you accept upgrade-time refactoring as a routine cost.

A vendor's distribution says it's "Kubernetes-compatible" but not "Certified Kubernetes." What does this difference signal?

answerCertified Kubernetes is a CNCF program with a specific test suite (run via hydrophone, historically sonobuoy) that the distribution has passed. "Kubernetes-compatible" is not a defined term — it might mean the distribution implements most of the Kubernetes API but has skipped or modified parts of it. The practical risk is portability: your manifests may work, or they may hit an unimplemented API and break in a way that wouldn't happen on a certified cluster. Prefer Certified Kubernetes when portability matters.
🎬 Choose Your Own Adventure

You read about a shiny new alpha feature and enable it cluster-wide. Your prod runs on it. Click to see what happens at the next minor upgrade. ▼

next minor upgradeThe alpha API was renamed without deprecation (legal under alpha rules). Your manifests don't apply. Pods stuck. You spend two weeks rewriting workloads. Lesson: alpha is the quiet branch line where breakage is expected. Production is the main intercity. Don't run intercity trains on branch-line tracks.
Words from this lesson · open if you want a quick reference
Borg
Google's internal cluster manager since the early 2000s. Paper published 2015. The grandfather of Kubernetes.
Omega
Google research successor to Borg (paper 2013). Explored shared-state scheduling. Many ideas became Kubernetes architecture.
CNCF
Cloud Native Computing Foundation. Linux Foundation project hosting Kubernetes and hundreds of related projects.
SIG
Special Interest Group. Sub-team within Kubernetes that owns an area (sig-network, sig-storage, sig-auth, etc.). Reviews KEPs in their area.
KEP
Kubernetes Enhancement Proposal. Markdown document in github.com/kubernetes/enhancements proposing a non-trivial change.
Feature gate
Per-feature on/off flag. Default depends on stage (alpha off, beta varies, GA on).
Alpha · Beta · GA
Three feature lifecycle stages. Alpha = unstable/dev-only. Beta = mostly stable, vet for prod. GA = stable, backward-compatible, default for production.
Conformance
CNCF Certified Kubernetes program. Distributions pass a test suite (hydrophone) to certify they implement the Kubernetes API correctly.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: Every Kubernetes feature graduates alpha → beta → GA, like train signals tested on a quiet branch line first. Production runs on GA.

✓ Lesson 07 complete · Module 1 done!

Borg → Omega → Kubernetes. CNCF homes it. ~3 minor releases per year. Every feature graduates alpha → beta → GA, like train signals tested on quiet branch lines first.

Next — Module 2 begins: Lesson 08 — Containers (deep dive). You've covered the foundations. Module 2 dives into what's actually inside a Pod: Linux primitives (namespaces, cgroups), OCI specs, runtimes (containerd, CRI-O, runc), image building, registries.

Suvis Guru · K-COM · Lesson 7 of 17 · Module 1 complete · grounded in kubernetes.io · cncf.io · research.google
Lesson 7-5· How a Linux Computer Works in 5 Minutes (primer)

📍 Today's stop in K-Town: Foundation Tour — a quick layman's loop before we dive into the technical districts.

📍 Foundation Tour · primerFoundation Tour primer, between Lessons 07 and 08.

Lesson 7.5 · Level-0 primer

How a Linux Computer Works in 5 Minutes

A quick primer before Module 2. Skip if you already know what a kernel is.

Module 2 (Lessons 08–12) explains how containers work at the Linux level. That conversation assumes you know what a process is, what an operating system is, what the kernel is, and what root means. If those words feel foggy, this 5-minute tour will make Lesson 08 land cleanly. If you already know them, skip ahead — go straight to Lesson 08.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: A container is a Linux process the kernel has been tricked into thinking it's alone on the machine. The next 5 minutes explain how the kernel does the trick.

Section 1 · the basics

What a computer actually does

A computer runs programs. A running program is called a process. Your laptop, right now, has hundreds of them — your browser, the clock in the menu bar, the wifi connection, the music app, a few dozen background helpers you've never named. Every one is a process.

A process is just instructions plus the memory it uses. Most of the time, processes are doing tiny amounts of work — checking for a notification, drawing a pixel, waiting for input — and the computer juggles them so quickly that they all feel simultaneous.

Section 2 · the traffic cop

What an operating system is

An operating system is software that decides which process gets the CPU, the memory, the disk, and when. It's the traffic cop of the machine. Linux is one. macOS is another. Windows is another. They all do the same job — share the hardware among many processes — using slightly different rules.

When you click an app icon, the operating system picks a CPU core for it, allocates some memory, gives it access to your files, and starts the process running. When the app needs to read a file or send a network packet, it asks the operating system to do it. Apps don't talk to the hardware directly; the operating system stands between them.

Section 3 · the heart

What the kernel is

The kernel is the heart of the operating system. The part that talks to the actual hardware (CPU, RAM, network card, disk). When a regular program wants something from the hardware — read a file, allocate memory, open a network connection — it asks the kernel. That ask is called a system call, or syscall for short.

Most of an operating system is utilities, drivers, and apps that sit around the kernel. The kernel itself is small, fast, and trusted with everything dangerous (talking to hardware, deciding who gets memory, enforcing security). When you "talk to Linux" you're really talking to the Linux kernel through syscalls.

Hardware at the bottom. Kernel sits on top of it. The operating system layer (utilities, libraries, services) sits on the kernel. Processes sit on top of all of that. When a process needs something from the hardware, it asks the kernel — that ask is a syscall.
Section 4 · the admin

What "root" means

Root is the administrator account on a Linux machine. Root can do anything — read any file, change any setting, install anything, kill any process. On macOS the equivalent is "admin." On Windows it's "Administrator." Same idea.

By default, most programs should NOT run as root. If a regular app gets compromised — a bug, an exploit, a malicious input — and it's running as root, the attacker has the keys to the whole machine. Modern operating systems are built around the assumption that apps run as a regular user, and only specifically-trusted things run as root (and even those only briefly).

You'll hear "non-root by default" a lot in Module 2. It's exactly this idea applied to containers — a containerized app should run as a regular user, not as root, so a bug in the app can't escalate to controlling the host.

Section 5 · the punchline

Why this matters for containers

Here's the punchline that makes Module 2 click: a container is a Linux process the kernel has been tricked into thinking it's alone on the machine.

That's it. There's no special "container" thing inside Linux. The kernel takes a regular process and gives it a private view of process IDs, files, network, and memory limits. The process can still ask for things — it still makes syscalls — but the kernel makes them look like the process owns the whole computer.

Module 2 is about the five Linux features the kernel uses to do that trick: namespaces (private views), cgroups (resource budgets), capabilities (fine-grained permissions instead of all-or-nothing root), seccomp (syscall filtering), and LSM (mandatory access control like AppArmor or SELinux). They were all added to Linux over years; Docker just made them easy to use together.

If you read this far, you have everything you need for Lesson 08. Onward.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: A container is a Linux process the kernel has been tricked into thinking it's alone on the machine. The next 5 minutes explain how the kernel does the trick.

✓ Foundation Tour complete

Programs run as processes. The operating system shares hardware among them. The kernel is the heart of the OS. Root is the administrator. A container is just a process with a private view.

Ready for Module 2? → Lesson 08: Linux Namespaces · cgroups · Capabilities.

Suvis Guru · K-COM · Foundation Tour primer · between Module 1 and Module 2
Lesson 08· Linux Namespaces · cgroups · Capabilities

📍 Today's stop in K-Town: Office Tower with Utility Meters.

📍 Office Tower · lesson 8 of 16Lesson 8 of 16, Office Tower with Utility Meters.

Module 2 · Lesson 08 · how containers actually work

Linux Namespaces · cgroups · Capabilities

A container is just a process with walls and meters — Linux features that make it think it's alone on the machine, with limits on how much it can use. No magic. Just well-named kernel features.

📎 New to Linux? Take a 5-minute detour through Lesson 7.5 — How a Linux Computer Works first.
🚨 The 3 AM Nightmare

Your container keeps getting killed. The logs say OOMKilled. You set the memory limit to 256Mi. The app reports it only uses 200MB. Why is it dying? Because the kernel — the real boss of the whole machine — is enforcing limits you didn't fully understand. This lesson is about the five Linux features that decide what your container can and can't do. No magic. Just plumbing.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: A container is a Linux process with walls (namespaces), meters (cgroups), and rules about what it can do (capabilities, seccomp, LSM). Five kernel features, no magic.

Section 1 · Concept

What's actually inside a container?

A container isn't a magical sandbox. It's a regular Linux process that the kernel tricks into thinking it's alone on the machine. Five Linux features make this work, and they all came from the kernel — not from Docker, not from Kubernetes. Docker just made them easy to use.

Namespaces give each container its own private view of system resources. Process IDs, network interfaces, mount points, hostnames, user IDs — they all look private to the container, but they're really shared kernel resources sliced into isolated views. There are 8 namespace types in modern Linux: PID, NET, MNT, UTS, IPC, USER, cgroup, and time.

cgroups (control groups) are the meters. Each container gets a budget for CPU, memory, disk I/O, and network bandwidth. Try to use more than your budget, and the kernel throttles you (or kills the process for memory).

[ deep dive — skip if new ]Two versions of cgroups exist — cgroup v1 (legacy, hierarchies per resource) and cgroup v2 (unified hierarchy, the modern default since ~2022). Kubernetes 1.25+ requires v2 for certain features (PSI-based memory pressure, the memory.high soft cap).

Capabilities chop the old "root vs not-root" model into ~40 fine-grained permissions. Instead of giving a container full root, you give it just NET_BIND_SERVICE (bind to port < 1024) or just SYS_TIME (change the clock). Containers should drop everything they don't need.

seccomp filters which Linux system calls a container is allowed to make. Modern container runtimes apply a default profile blocking roughly 40–65 dangerous syscalls (varies by runtime version) out of ~300 total. You can write custom profiles for tighter restriction.

AppArmor / SELinux are mandatory access control (MAC) systems. They define explicit policies — "this container can read these files but not those" — at a layer below normal Linux permissions. Different distros pick different ones (Ubuntu/Debian default to AppArmor; RHEL/Fedora default to SELinux).

That's it. Five Linux features. Stack them together and you have a container. Take them away and a container is just a normal process.

Section 1.5 · The 5 Linux primitives, broken down

Each primitive, one tile

Five kernel features. Together they make a container.

🚪

1 · Namespaces

private rooms (8 of them)

Private views of process IDs, network, mounts, hostname, etc. Container sees only its own slice. 8 types in modern Linux.

⚡

2 · cgroups

meters (CPU · RAM · I/O)

Resource budget per container. Exceed CPU? throttled. Exceed memory? OOM-killed. Two versions: v1 (legacy) and v2 (modern unified).

🔑

3 · Capabilities

root, but in 40 small pieces

Root used to be all-or-nothing. Now: 40+ fine-grained capabilities (NET_BIND_SERVICE, SYS_TIME, etc.). Drop everything you don't need.

🛂

4 · seccomp

syscall whitelist

Filters which Linux system calls a container can make. Default profile blocks roughly 40–65 dangerous syscalls (varies by runtime version) out of ~300. Custom profiles available.

🛡️

5 · AppArmor / SELinux

mandatory access control

Defines explicit policies — "this container can read these files but not those." Below normal permissions. Ubuntu = AppArmor, RHEL = SELinux.

Stack all five and you have a container. Remove them and you just have a regular Linux process. No magic. Just kernel features.

Section 1.7 · The 8 namespaces, in detail

One row per namespace type

These are the 8 namespace types Linux ships today. Each gives a container a private view of one specific kind of resource.

PID
Own process ID tree. Container sees its own PID 1, 2, 3 — even though those are different real PIDs on the host.
NET
Own network interfaces, IP addresses, routing table, firewall rules. Each container has its own private network stack.
MNT
Own filesystem mount table. Container sees its own root filesystem (built from image layers).
UTS
Own hostname and domain name. Each container can have its own hostname like a real machine.
IPC
Own inter-process communication (System V IPC, POSIX message queues). Containers can't talk to each other through shared memory by default.
USER
Own user/group ID mapping. Container's UID 0 (root) can map to host UID 100000 (unprivileged). Powerful for security.
cgroup
Own view of the cgroup hierarchy. Container sees its own cgroup root, not the host's.
time
Newest. Own monotonic and boot clocks (not wall clock). Useful for testing time-sensitive code in containers.

When you create a container, the runtime calls clone() with flags like CLONE_NEWPID | CLONE_NEWNET | CLONE_NEWNS to put the new process in fresh namespaces. That single syscall is the moment a "container" comes into existence.

⏸ Pause and check

What stops one container from seeing another container's processes?

Answer: b. Each container gets a private view of process IDs. Without that namespace, ps would show every process on the host.

Section 2 · Before & After

Without these primitives vs with them

Linux had multitasking forever — multiple processes could run on the same machine. But until namespaces and cgroups, "isolation" meant Unix permissions and prayer. Containers required the kernel to grow new tools.

Same machine, same processes. Namespaces give them privacy. cgroups give them budgets.
Before · pre-2008

Multiple processes on one Linux machine shared everything: same PID space, same network, same filesystem, same hostname. Process A could see B's PIDs, ports, files. If A started a runaway loop, it stole all the CPU. Isolation was Unix permissions and prayer.

Companies that wanted real isolation used VMs — heavy, slow to start, but each VM had its own OS so isolation was guaranteed.

After · namespaces + cgroups

Linux added namespaces (private views) and cgroups (resource budgets) over the late 2000s. By 2013, Docker showed the world how to combine them into something usable. Now multiple processes can run on the same kernel but think they're alone, with hard limits on CPU and memory.

That's a container. Process-level isolation, milliseconds to start, hundreds per host. Cheaper than VMs. Better isolation than mere Unix permissions.

What didn't change: containers still share the kernel. A kernel exploit affects everything. For untrusted multi-tenancy, you still want VMs or sandboxed runtimes (gVisor, Kata).

Section 3 · Analogy

An office building, with utility meters per office

Think of a 4-story office building. Many tenants share the same building — same foundation, same plumbing, same electricity. But each tenant rents a private office: their own door, their own desk, their own walls, their own filing cabinet. From inside, it feels like their own space. They don't trip over the next tenant's stuff.

The building has a utility meter for each office. Office A pays for the electricity it uses. Office B has its own meter. The landlord caps how much power each office can pull, so one office running a giant printer doesn't blow the whole building's circuit. There's a security guard at each door checking IDs to make sure only the right people get in.

Linux containers work exactly the same way. Namespaces are the office walls (private views of resources). cgroups are the utility meters (resource budgets). Capabilities and seccomp and AppArmor/SELinux are the security guards (which actions are allowed). The building (the host kernel) is shared by everyone, but each container thinks it's alone.

In the story……in Kubernetes
The office buildingThe host machine + Linux kernel
Each officeA container (a process or process group)
Office walls and a private doorLinux namespaces (PID, NET, MNT, UTS, IPC, USER, cgroup, time)
Utility meters per officecgroups (CPU, memory, I/O budgets)
The doorman / security guardcapabilities + seccomp + AppArmor / SELinux
Shared building utilitiesThe host kernel everyone shares

⚠️ The analogy stops here: an office building has one electrical panel that one tenant can overload. The kernel can be DoS'd by a noisy container too — but cgroups make this much harder than the real-world equivalent.

Section 4 · Two-level explanation

Two ways to hear it

Same machinery, two depths.

ELI5 · explain like I'm 5

Imagine a big shared playroom. Linux puts up little curtains so each kid has their own pretend room. Each kid has a snack bowl with rules on how many cookies they can eat. And there's a teacher checking what they're allowed to do. The kids share the same playroom floor (the kernel) but they each think they have their own little space.

ELI10 · explain like I'm 10

A container is just a regular Linux process with three things added by the kernel: namespaces (it sees its own private slice of process IDs, network, files, hostname, etc.), cgroups (it has hard limits on CPU, memory, disk I/O), and security policies (capabilities, seccomp, AppArmor/SELinux — fine-grained control over what kernel actions it can perform). When you "start a container" with Docker or containerd, the runtime calls clone() with namespace flags, configures cgroups, drops most capabilities, and applies a seccomp profile. The result is a process that thinks it's alone on the machine. Hundreds of these can run on one host because they all share the host's kernel — way more efficient than VMs.

⏸ Pause and check

A container hits 257MB of memory; its limits.memory is 256Mi. What happens?

Answer: b. cgroup memory limits aren't suggestions. The kernel enforces them with OOMKilled.

Section 5 · Real-world scenarios

Where these primitives matter in practice

Four real situations where understanding namespaces, cgroups, or capabilities saves the day.

Container OOM-killed at 256 MiB

An engineer notices their Pod keeps restarting. kubectl describe Pod shows reason: OOMKilled. They had set memory limit to 256Mi in the Pod spec. The app legitimately needed 350Mi at peak. The kernel's cgroup-v2 memory controller saw memory usage exceed the cgroup's limit and killed PID 1 in the container — exactly as designed. Fix: bump the limit. The lesson: cgroup limits aren't suggestions. The kernel will kill you.

Container can't bind to port 80

A developer's containerized web app fails: permission denied binding to port 80. The container is running as a non-root user (good practice). Ports below 1024 require CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE on Linux. By default, modern container runtimes drop this capability. Fix: either run on port 8080 and let the load balancer rewrite, or explicitly add NET_BIND_SERVICE to the container's capability set. The lesson: container runtimes drop capabilities aggressively for safety; you have to explicitly opt back in.

Container A can see container B's processes

A security audit finds container A can see container B's process list via ps. Investigation: someone set hostPID: true in container A's Pod spec, sharing the host's PID namespace. This was a debugging convenience that got merged. Fix: remove hostPID: true. The lesson: every "host*" Pod field (hostNetwork, hostPID, hostIPC) drops a namespace and gives the container access to host resources. Use sparingly, audit regularly.

Custom seccomp profile for a sandboxed workload

A team runs untrusted user code (a code-execution playground). The default seccomp profile blocks roughly 40–65 dangerous syscalls (varies by runtime version) but still allows things like ptrace. They write a custom seccomp profile that blocks ptrace, ioctl, and several others. The user code can still run normal Python; it can't escape the sandbox. The lesson: seccomp default profiles are good baseline; for hostile workloads, write a tighter custom profile.

Section 6 · Animation

Watch a container come into existence

Three modes. The first shows the kernel building a container step-by-step. The second shows what happens when a container hits its memory limit. The third shows host-namespace sharing.

Watching: the kernel constructs a container by adding namespaces, cgroups, capabilities, and seccomp — one feature at a time.
Section 7 · Misconceptions, flashcards & quiz

Lock it in

Three common misconceptions to clear up first, then the flashcards and quiz.

Common Misconceptions

Myth: "Container" is a Linux feature.
Truth: Containers are made by combining five kernel features (namespaces + cgroups + capabilities + seccomp + LSM). The runtime (Docker, containerd) bundles them. The kernel does the work.
Myth: Each container has its own kernel.
Truth: All containers on a host share the host's kernel. That's the whole point — and the whole risk. A kernel exploit can break out of any container.
Myth: limits and requests are the same thing.
Truth: requests is what the scheduler reserves (the floor). limits is the hard ceiling (CPU throttled, memory OOM-killed above).
What is a container, technically?
tap to flip
A regular Linux process placed in fresh namespaces (private views), constrained by cgroups (resource budgets), and restricted by capabilities + seccomp + LSM (security policies). All kernel features.
Name the 8 namespace types
tap to flip
PID, NET, MNT, UTS, IPC, USER, cgroup, time. Each gives a private view of one specific kind of resource.
cgroups v1 vs v2?
tap to flip
v1: separate hierarchies per resource (CPU hierarchy, memory hierarchy, etc.). v2: unified single hierarchy. v2 is the modern default since ~2022 (Kubernetes 1.25+ requires it on certain features).
What are Linux capabilities?
tap to flip
~40 fine-grained permissions chopping the old "root vs not-root" model. Examples: NET_BIND_SERVICE (bind to port < 1024), SYS_TIME (change clock), SYS_ADMIN (most-dangerous, do not grant). Drop everything you don't need.
What is seccomp?
tap to flip
Linux feature filtering which system calls a process can make. Modern container runtimes apply a default profile blocking roughly 40–65 dangerous syscalls (varies by runtime version). Custom profiles allow tighter restriction for sensitive workloads.
AppArmor vs SELinux?
tap to flip
Both are Linux Security Modules (LSM) providing mandatory access control. AppArmor is path-based, simpler. SELinux is label-based, more powerful but harder. Ubuntu/Debian default to AppArmor; RHEL/Fedora default to SELinux.
What does hostPID: true do?
tap to flip
Disables the PID namespace for the container, sharing the host's PID namespace instead. Container can see all host processes via ps. Useful for debugging, dangerous for security. Same pattern: hostNetwork, hostIPC.
Why is the kernel shared?
tap to flip
Containers are processes on the host kernel — that's the whole point of containers vs VMs. It makes them lightweight (no boot time, no OS overhead per container). The trade-off: a kernel exploit can break out of any container. For untrusted multi-tenancy, use VMs or sandboxed runtimes (gVisor, Kata).

A container running as non-root user keeps failing with "permission denied" when binding to port 80. What's happening, and what are two fixes?

answerPorts below 1024 require CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE on Linux. Modern container runtimes drop this capability by default for security. Two fixes: (1) bind to port 8080+ and let your load balancer/Service map external port 80 to internal 8080 (preferred — keeps the container low-privilege). (2) Explicitly add NET_BIND_SERVICE to the container's capability set in the Pod spec under securityContext.capabilities.add. The first is the cleaner pattern.

A Pod has memory limit 256Mi and the container keeps getting OOMKilled. The app reports it only uses ~200MB. Why might it still be OOMKilled?

answerThe cgroup memory accounting includes more than the app's heap: page cache, anonymous mappings, kernel memory, etc. The 256Mi limit is enforced on the cgroup's total memory.usage_in_bytes, which includes everything the container has caused the kernel to allocate. Common causes of "I only use 200MB but it's OOMKilled at 256MB": JVM/Node off-heap, large file caches, memory-mapped files. Fix: bump the limit or set memory.high (cgroup v2) for soft pressure before the hard kill.
🎬 Choose Your Own Adventure

A teammate adds hostNetwork: true to a Pod spec "just to test something." Click to see what they just did. ▼

what they just didThe container now shares the host's network stack — same IPs, same interfaces, same firewall rules. It can bind to any host port (and conflict with anything else on the host). It can sniff traffic on host interfaces. K8s Service discovery often breaks because the Pod has the host's IP, not a Pod IP. Lesson: every host* field (hostNetwork, hostPID, hostIPC) drops a namespace and gives the container access to host resources. Powerful for specific cases (CNI plugins). Footgun for normal apps.
Words from this lesson · open if you want a quick reference
Namespace
Linux kernel feature giving a process a private view of one resource type. 8 types: PID, NET, MNT, UTS, IPC, USER, cgroup, time.
cgroup (control group)
Linux feature limiting how much CPU, memory, I/O, and other resources a process group can use. Two versions: v1 (legacy) and v2 (modern).
Capability
One of ~40 fine-grained permissions in Linux. Examples: NET_BIND_SERVICE, SYS_TIME, SYS_ADMIN. Replaces the old all-or-nothing root model.
seccomp
Secure Computing Mode. Filters which Linux syscalls a process can make. Container runtimes apply default profiles blocking roughly 40–65 dangerous syscalls (varies by runtime version).
AppArmor
Linux Security Module providing mandatory access control via path-based policies. Default on Ubuntu/Debian.
SELinux
Linux Security Module providing mandatory access control via label-based policies. Default on RHEL/Fedora.
OOMKilled
"Out of memory killed." When a container exceeds its memory cgroup limit, the kernel kills its PID 1. Pods restart per their restart policy.
hostNetwork / hostPID / hostIPC
Pod-spec fields that disable the corresponding namespace, sharing the host's. Useful for specific cases; security concern for normal workloads.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: A container is a Linux process with walls (namespaces), meters (cgroups), and rules about what it can do (capabilities, seccomp, LSM). Five kernel features, no magic.

✓ Lesson 08 complete · Module 2 begins!

A container is a Linux process with namespaces (privacy), cgroups (budgets), and security policies (capabilities, seccomp, LSM). Five kernel features. No magic.

Next — Lesson 09: Container Runtimes & the OCI standard. What containerd, CRI-O, runc, crun, and youki actually do. Image formats, layers, manifests, registries.

Suvis Guru · K-COM · Lesson 8 of 17 · Module 2 begins · grounded in Linux man pages · kernel.org docs
Lesson 09· Container Runtimes & OCI

📍 Today's stop in K-Town: Customs Warehouse.

📍 Customs Warehouse · lesson 9 of 16Lesson 9 of 16, Customs Warehouse.

Module 2 · Lesson 09 · the standard format + the chefs that read it

Container Runtimes & OCI Standard

An OCI image is a standardized box. A registry is a warehouse. A runtime is the forklift that pulls a box and turns it into a running container. Many forklifts. One standard.

🚨 The 3 AM Nightmare

Your team built an app on an ARM Mac (M-series). You deploy to AMD64 nodes in EKS. The Pod crashes immediately: exec format error. You can't tell from the message that the binary is the wrong architecture. You stare at logs for an hour. This lesson is about the format that makes containers portable — and the standard that lets one image work on every CPU and every cloud.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: OCI is the standard. Runtimes are forklifts. Layers stack. Tags move; digests don't. Pin to digests in production.

Section 1 · Concept

The standard, the runtimes, and the storage

In 2015, the container ecosystem was at risk of fragmenting — Docker had its own format, CoreOS had rkt with a different format, and every cloud was building incompatible variations. The industry agreed to a common standard hosted by the Linux Foundation: OCI (Open Container Initiative). Today, every serious container tool implements OCI specs.

OCI defines three specifications: the image spec (what's in a container image — layers, manifest, config), the runtime spec (how to start a container from an unpacked filesystem), and the distribution spec (how registries serve images via HTTP). Together they form a complete contract that lets you build with one tool, store in any registry, and run with any runtime.

[ deep dive — skip if new ]Container runtimes are the programs that actually start containers. Two layers exist: a high-level runtime like containerd or CRI-O handles image pulling, lifecycle, and the Kubernetes CRI (Container Runtime Interface). A low-level runtime like runc, crun, or youki does the actual clone() + cgroups + capabilities setup we saw in Lesson 08. runc is the reference implementation (Go); crun is a faster C rewrite; youki is a Rust rewrite.

An image is a stack of layers (read-only filesystem snapshots), a manifest (JSON listing the layers + their digests), and a config (how to run it: command, env, ports). When the runtime "runs an image," it stacks those layers into a unified filesystem, adds a writable top layer, and starts the container.

Tags are mutable friendly names like nginx:1.27 or my-app:latest. They point at images. A tag can be moved tomorrow. A digest is a cryptographic hash like sha256:abc123… that identifies a specific image content. Same content = same digest, always. For reproducibility and supply-chain safety, prefer digests in production.

Registries are HTTP services that store and serve images. Big public ones: Docker Hub, GitHub Container Registry (GHCR), AWS ECR, Google Artifact Registry, Azure ACR, Red Hat Quay, JFrog Container Registry, Harbor (open-source self-hosted). All speak the OCI distribution spec, so the same image works across them.

Section 1.5 · The 5 pieces, broken down

Each piece, one tile

OCI · Runtimes · Layers · Tags vs Digests · Registries.

📜

1 · OCI specs

the standard contract

Three specs from the Open Container Initiative (Linux Foundation, 2015): image format, runtime behavior, distribution protocol.

🚜

2 · Runtimes

containerd · runc · crun · youki

High-level (containerd) handles images + lifecycle + CRI. Low-level (runc, crun, youki) does the actual kernel calls.

📦

3 · Layers + manifest

stacked, content-addressed

Each layer is a tarball with its own SHA256. Manifest lists them. Container image = layers + manifest + config.

🏷️

4 · Tags vs digests

friendly name vs fingerprint

Tag = mutable label that points at a digest. Digest = SHA256 of the manifest, immutable. For reproducibility, pin to digest.

🏬

5 · Registries

where images live

HTTP services serving images. Public: Docker Hub, GHCR. Cloud: ECR (AWS), GCR/AR (GCP), ACR (Azure). Self-host: Harbor, Quay.

All five interlock: OCI defines the format · runtimes execute it · layers structure it · digests identify it · registries store it.

Section 1.7 · The runtime landscape (2026)

Who's who in the runtime world

Five tools you'll hear about. They split into two layers — the high-level runtime that talks to Kubernetes, and the low-level runtime that talks to the kernel.

containerd
High-level · default in K8s
CNCF graduated. Default container runtime in most Kubernetes distributions. Handles image pulling, container lifecycle, CRI for kubelet.
CRI-O
High-level · K8s-only
Built specifically for Kubernetes (no general-purpose API). Smaller than containerd. Common on OpenShift and bare-metal K8s.
runc
Low-level · reference
The original OCI runtime. Written in Go. Default low-level runtime under containerd and CRI-O. Reference implementation everyone trusts.
crun
Low-level · faster
C rewrite of runc by Red Hat. Smaller binary, lower memory, faster startup. Drop-in replacement for runc on most distros.
youki
Low-level · Rust
Rust rewrite of runc. Memory-safe. Newer (gaining maturity). OCI-compatible drop-in. Picked up where speed + safety matter.
gVisor / Kata
Sandboxed runtimes
Heavier-isolation alternatives. gVisor intercepts syscalls in user space; Kata runs each container in a tiny VM. Used for hostile multi-tenancy.

Most Kubernetes clusters run containerd + runc by default. You generally don't need to think about runtime choice unless you have specific isolation or performance requirements.

Section 1.8 · Tag vs digest · the most important distinction

Why production should pin to digests

This one trips up almost everyone. Read both cards.

TAG
nginx:1.27

Mutable friendly name. The tag is a label that points at an image. The owner of the image can move the tag tomorrow.

Today nginx:1.27 might point at one image. Next week, after a CVE fix, it points at a different image (with the same "1.27" version number). That's a feature for ops (auto-updating patches) but a footgun for reproducibility.

⚠ :latest is the worst offender. Avoid in production.

DIGEST
nginx@sha256:abc123def456…

Immutable cryptographic fingerprint. The SHA256 hash of the image manifest. Same content = same digest, forever. Different content = different digest. Cannot be moved.

In production, pinning to a digest guarantees you'll get exactly the bits you tested. CI builds an image, computes its digest, deploys that digest. The deployment is now reproducible — six months later, the same digest still resolves to the same bits.

✓ Use in production. Get bytes, not vibes.

In Kubernetes manifests, write image: nginx@sha256:abc... instead of image: nginx:1.27. Tools like Renovate and Dependabot can auto-update digests via PR — keeping reproducibility AND staying current.

⏸ Pause and check

What does "OCI" stand for?

Answer: b. Three specs (image, runtime, distribution). The contract that lets any runtime work with any registry.

Section 2 · Before & After

Before OCI vs after

OCI didn't make containers possible — it made them portable. Before the standard, every tool had its own format and no two tools could read each other's images.

Same idea (containers), different freedom levels. OCI standardization unlocked the ecosystem.
Before · pre-OCI (2015)

Docker images only worked with Docker. CoreOS rkt had its own format. Cloud vendors built proprietary variants. Switching tools meant rebuilding everything — different image format, different registry protocol, different runtime API.

After · OCI standard

One image format works with any runtime (containerd, CRI-O, Podman). One registry protocol means any image hosts on any registry (Docker Hub, GHCR, ECR, GCR, Harbor). The image you build today still works in 5 years on a runtime that doesn't exist yet — as long as it's OCI-compliant.

What didn't change: there's still operational complexity around image signing, supply-chain security (SBOMs, provenance), and registry credential management. OCI standardized the format, not the policy.

Section 3 · Analogy

Standardized warehouses and forklifts

Imagine the global shipping industry. There's one standard for shipping containers (ISO sizes, lifting points, manifest format). Any container fits on any truck, ship, or train. Any warehouse can store any container. Any forklift can pick one up. The standard is what makes the network work.

OCI is that standard for software containers. The image format is the standard "container size." The distribution spec is how warehouses communicate. The runtime spec is what the forklift does to pick a box up and get it moving. Different forklifts (runc, crun, youki) all work with the same boxes. Different warehouses (Docker Hub, GHCR, ECR) all store the same boxes.

Inside each box, there are smaller boxes stacked together — the layers. The manifest is the packing list nailed to the side. The tag is the friendly label ("v1.27") and the digest is the unique tracking number. You can move the friendly label, but the tracking number stays with the box forever.

In the story……in Kubernetes
A standard ISO shipping containerAn OCI image (the format)
Layers stacked inside the containerImage layers (each a tarball with its own SHA-256)
The packing list on the doorThe image manifest (JSON describing the layers)
A friendly label on the outsideA tag (mutable — can be moved by the publisher)
The unique tracking numberThe digest (immutable SHA-256 hash of the manifest)
The warehouseA container registry (Docker Hub, GHCR, ECR, GCR, ACR, Harbor, Quay)
The forklifts that move boxesContainer runtimes (containerd / CRI-O at high level; runc / crun / youki at low level)

⚠️ The analogy stops here: a real shipping container has fixed dimensions. A software image is whatever size the layers add up to. The "standard" is the format, not the size.

Section 4 · Two-level explanation

Two ways to hear it

ELI5 · explain like I'm 5

There are giant warehouses full of boxes. Every box is the same shape, with a label on the outside and a packing list inside. Forklifts can pick up any box from any warehouse, because all the boxes are the same shape. Some boxes have stickers like "v1.27" — those stickers can be moved. Some boxes have a special bar code that nobody can change. That's how containers work too.

ELI10 · explain like I'm 10

OCI (Open Container Initiative) is a Linux Foundation standard with three specs: image format (what's in a container image), runtime spec (how to start a container from an unpacked filesystem), and distribution spec (how registries serve images over HTTP). Container runtimes split into high-level (containerd, CRI-O — pull images, manage lifecycle, talk to Kubernetes via CRI) and low-level (runc, crun, youki — make the kernel clone() + cgroups + capabilities calls from Lesson 08). Images are stacks of read-only layers + a manifest. Tags like nginx:1.27 are mutable labels; digests like sha256:abc... are immutable hashes. For production reproducibility, pin to digests.

⏸ Pause and check

You see nginx:1.27 and nginx@sha256:abc…. Which is safe to use in production?

Answer: b. Tags can be moved by the publisher. Digests are SHA-256 of the manifest — same content always = same digest. Pin to digests for reproducibility.

Section 5 · Real-world scenarios

Where OCI, runtimes, and registries trip people up

"latest" tag broke production

A team ran image: my-app:latest in their Kubernetes Deployments. Friday at 6 PM, someone published a new "latest" with a bad migration. Pods restarting through the night picked up the new "latest" tag. By Saturday morning, half the Pods had the bad image and half had the old one. The fix: pin to digests, and never use :latest in production manifests. Renovate auto-updates digests via PR (with tests + review).

Multi-arch image build (one tag, two architectures)

A team builds an app that needs to run on both ARM (Graviton EC2) and AMD64 (Intel laptops). They use docker buildx with --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64. The result is a single tag my-app:v2 that contains two manifests — one per arch. When a node pulls the image, the runtime picks the right manifest. Same tag, right binary on every machine.

Switched runtime from runc to crun for performance

A high-density container platform (200+ containers per host) switched from runc to crun. Crun's smaller memory footprint and faster startup added up across thousands of containers — Pod startup latency dropped by ~30%, host memory savings of ~5%. The change: edit containerd config to set the runtime path. Application teams noticed nothing — same OCI image, just a different forklift underneath.

Image pull failed: ImagePullBackOff (private registry auth)

A team pushed their image to a private GHCR repo. Deploying it failed with ErrImagePull → ImagePullBackOff. Investigation: the kubelet didn't have credentials for GHCR. Fix: create a Kubernetes Secret of type kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson with the GHCR token, and reference it in the Pod spec via imagePullSecrets. Or use a node-level credential provider for cleaner ops at scale.

Section 6 · Animation

Watch a container image become a running container

Three modes. The first shows the pull → unpack → run sequence. The second shows tag mutation (why digests matter). The third shows the runtime stack from kubelet down to kernel.

Watching: the runtime pulls a manifest, fetches each layer in parallel, unpacks them as a stack, then starts the container.
Section 7 · Misconceptions, flashcards & quiz

Lock it in

Three common misconceptions to clear up first, then the flashcards and quiz.

Common Misconceptions

Myth: Docker is the only container runtime.
Truth: Modern Kubernetes runs containerd or CRI-O by default. Docker is a higher-level platform that uses containerd underneath.
Myth: :latest is fine for production.
Truth: :latest is the worst tag offender. It's mutable. Two Pods with the same :latest tag can run different code on different nodes after a re-push. Pin to a digest.
Myth: An image works on any CPU.
Truth: Only if it was built multi-arch (docker buildx --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64). Otherwise an ARM build will not run on AMD64 nodes.
What does OCI stand for and what does it do?
tap to flip
Open Container Initiative — Linux Foundation project (2015) that defines three specs: image format, runtime behavior, and distribution protocol. The contract that lets any runtime + any registry work together.
High-level vs low-level runtime?
tap to flip
High-level (containerd, CRI-O): handles image pulling, container lifecycle, talks to Kubernetes via CRI. Low-level (runc, crun, youki): does the actual kernel calls — clone(), cgroups, capabilities, seccomp.
runc vs crun vs youki?
tap to flip
runc = original Go reference implementation. crun = Red Hat C rewrite, smaller and faster. youki = Rust rewrite, memory-safe, newer. All OCI-compliant drop-in replacements.
What's inside an OCI image?
tap to flip
A stack of layers (read-only filesystem snapshots, each a tarball with its own SHA256), a manifest (JSON listing layers and their digests), and a config (how to run: command, env, ports, user).
Tag vs digest?
tap to flip
Tag (nginx:1.27) = mutable friendly name. Can be moved by the publisher. Digest (sha256:abc...) = immutable cryptographic fingerprint. Same content always = same digest. For reproducibility, pin to digests in production.
What is multi-arch?
tap to flip
A single image tag containing multiple manifests — one per architecture (linux/amd64, linux/arm64, etc.). When pulled, the runtime picks the right one for the host. Built with docker buildx --platform.
Name 5 registries
Public: Docker Hub, GitHub Container Registry (GHCR). Cloud: AWS ECR, Google Artifact Registry (GCR/AR), Azure Container Registry (ACR). Self-host: Harbor, Quay. All OCI-compliant.
What is ImagePullBackOff?
tap to flip
Kubernetes Pod state when the kubelet repeatedly fails to pull an image. Common causes: typo in image name, wrong tag, missing registry credentials (need imagePullSecrets), or registry rate-limiting (Docker Hub anonymous limits).

A teammate says "let's just use :latest for our production deployments — it'll auto-update us to the newest version." What do you say?

answerIt's a footgun. :latest is a mutable tag — the image owner can move it at any time. When Pods restart in production (which happens constantly), they'll pull whatever :latest happens to point at then, not what you tested with. Half your fleet may end up on a different image than the other half. Pin to digests (nginx@sha256:abc...) in production manifests, and use Renovate or Dependabot to bump digests via PR (with tests). You get reproducibility AND stay current.

A Pod stays in ImagePullBackOff. kubectl describe Pod shows the kubelet is trying to pull from ghcr.io/myorg/my-app:v2 but failing with "denied". What's the likely fix?

answerMissing or wrong registry credentials. GHCR requires authentication for private images. Fix: create a Kubernetes Secret of type kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson containing GHCR auth (a personal access token with read:packages, or a GitHub App token), then reference it in the Pod spec via imagePullSecrets: [{name: ghcr-creds}]. For larger fleets, use a node-level credential provider (cleaner ops than per-namespace secrets).
🎬 Choose Your Own Adventure

Your team uses image: my-app:latest in production "so we always get the newest version." Click to see what happens on Friday at 6 PM. ▼

by Saturday morningSomeone pushes a new latest with a bad migration. Pods restarting through the night pick up the new image. Half the Pods are on the bad image and half on the old — depending on when each Pod last restarted. You can't even reproduce the issue locally because latest is whatever it is right now. Fix: pin to a digest. Use Renovate to bump digests via PR with tests. Reproducibility AND staying current.
Words from this lesson · open if you want a quick reference
OCI
Open Container Initiative. Linux Foundation project (2015). Defines image, runtime, and distribution specs that all container tools follow.
containerd
CNCF graduated high-level container runtime. Default in most Kubernetes clusters. Handles image pulling and lifecycle.
CRI-O
Kubernetes-only high-level runtime, smaller than containerd. Common on OpenShift.
runc / crun / youki
Low-level OCI runtimes that do the actual kernel calls. runc = Go reference. crun = C, faster. youki = Rust, memory-safe.
Image manifest
JSON document listing the layers (with their digests), config, and metadata of an OCI image.
Tag
Mutable friendly label like nginx:1.27 or my-app:latest. Points at a specific image; can be moved by publisher.
Digest
Immutable SHA256 hash of the image manifest. Same content = same digest. Use for reproducibility.
Registry
HTTP service speaking the OCI distribution spec, storing and serving images. Examples: Docker Hub, GHCR, ECR, GCR, ACR, Harbor.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: OCI is the standard. Runtimes are forklifts. Layers stack. Tags move; digests don't. Pin to digests in production.

✓ Lesson 09 complete

OCI is the standard. Runtimes are forklifts. Layers stack into a unified filesystem. Tags move; digests don't. Registries store and serve.

Next — Lesson 10: Image Building. How images are actually built — Dockerfiles, BuildKit, Buildah, multi-stage builds, distroless, scratch, multi-arch, rootless builds. Plus security: SBOMs, image signing, CVEs.
