Suvis Guru · K-COM · Lesson 9 of 17 · grounded in opencontainers.org · containerd.io · github.com/opencontainers
Lesson 10· Image Building · Multi-stage · Distroless · SBOM

📍 Today's stop in K-Town: Bakery District.

📍 Bakery District · lesson 10 of 16Lesson 10 of 16, Bakery District.

Module 2 · Lesson 10 · how images are baked

Image Building · Multi-Stage · Distroless · SBOM & Signing

Building an image is like baking a cake. Recipe (Dockerfile) + oven (builder) + ingredients (base + layers) → a finished cake. The pros use less sugar, sign their work, and ship a list of every ingredient.

🚨 The 3 AM Nightmare

Your Node.js image is 1.2 GB. CVE scans flag 124 vulnerabilities, mostly in libraries you don't even use. Production pulls take 90 seconds — that's 90 seconds of extra outage every time something restarts. Your security team is asking pointed questions. This lesson is about the changes that take that image from 1.2 GB to 80 MB and from 124 CVEs to 8 — without touching your app code.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: Build small (multi-stage), ship smaller (distroless), sign your work, generate an SBOM, scan for CVEs. That's a production-grade image.

Section 1 · Concept

How a container image actually gets built

An image is not magic. You write a Dockerfile (or equivalent) describing the build steps, hand it to a builder, and out comes an OCI image. Each build instruction (FROM, COPY, RUN) becomes a layer. The builder hashes everything, deduplicates, and pushes to a registry.

Build tools beyond Docker. Docker's BuildKit is the dominant builder, but several alternatives exist. Buildah (Red Hat) builds images without a daemon. Kaniko (Google) builds images inside a container itself, useful for CI in Kubernetes. ko builds Go images without a Dockerfile (just point it at your Go module). Cloud Native Buildpacks (Heroku/Pivotal) detect your language and build automatically — no Dockerfile.

Multi-stage builds are the most important Dockerfile pattern. You have one stage that builds your app (compiler, dev dependencies, full toolchain) and another that copies only the artifacts into a tiny final image. The builder is huge; the runtime is small.

Base image strategies. Three trade-offs: full distros like ubuntu (large, full shell, every tool), slim variants like node:22-alpine (smaller, fewer tools), distroless images (just your app + minimal runtime, no shell, no package manager), and scratch (literally empty — just your statically-linked binary). Each strip-down reduces image size and attack surface.

[ deep dive — skip if new ]Multi-arch with buildx. Build one image that works on both AMD64 and ARM64 with docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64. The result is a single tag containing manifests for each architecture (we covered this in Lesson 09). Under the hood buildx uses QEMU emulation to cross-compile per platform inside the build container.

Supply chain. Three concerns. CVEs — known vulnerabilities in the image's components. Tools like Trivy, Grype, Snyk scan your image and flag them. SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) — a machine-readable list of everything inside your image, in formats like SPDX or CycloneDX. Image signing — cryptographically prove the image came from your build pipeline (cosign, sigstore, Notary v2). Together they form your image supply-chain story.

Section 1.5 · The 5 building blocks, broken down

Each topic, one tile

📜

1 · Dockerfile + alternatives

recipes for images

Dockerfile is the dominant format. Alternatives: Buildah, Kaniko, ko (Go), Cloud Native Buildpacks (no Dockerfile).

🏗️

2 · Multi-stage

build big, ship small

One Dockerfile, two stages. Builder has compilers + dev deps. Runtime has only the compiled artifact. 10-100× smaller final image.

📐

3 · Base images

distro · alpine · distroless · scratch

Pick the smallest base that works. Distroless = no shell, no apt. Scratch = literally nothing. Smaller = faster + safer.

🌍

4 · Multi-arch (buildx)

one image, many CPUs

docker buildx --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 builds one tag with two manifests. Right binary on every node automatically.

🛡️

5 · SBOM · Sign · Scan

supply chain

SBOM = list of every ingredient. Signing = baker's seal (cosign). Scanning = inspector finds known CVEs (Trivy, Grype).

All five compound: good Dockerfile + multi-stage + small base + multi-arch + signed = production-grade image.

Section 1.7 · The build tool landscape

Pick the right builder for the job

Five tools you'll see in the wild. Most teams use BuildKit (the default Docker builder) and never look further. Specialized cases use the others.

BuildKit
Default · Docker
Modern Docker builder. Parallelism, layer caching, secret mounts. The default behind docker build. Use unless you have a reason not to.
Buildah
Red Hat · daemonless
Builds OCI images without a daemon. Doesn't need privileged container. Common on RHEL / OpenShift CI.
Kaniko
Google · in-cluster
Builds images inside an unprivileged container. Perfect for Kubernetes-based CI (Tekton, Argo Workflows). No Docker socket needed.
ko
Go · no Dockerfile
Builds images directly from Go modules — no Dockerfile, no Docker daemon. Tiny images. Used by major K8s projects.
Buildpacks
Heroku · auto-detect
Cloud Native Buildpacks detect your language (Python, Node, Java, Go, etc.) and build an image automatically. No Dockerfile needed. Used by Heroku, Cloud Foundry, Cloud Run.
Bazel · Nix
Reproducible
Bytewise-reproducible image builds. Heavy investment, big payoff for orgs that need every build to produce identical bits.

For 90% of teams: BuildKit. For Kubernetes-based CI: Kaniko. For Go: ko. For "just build my Python app please": Buildpacks.

Section 1.8 · Base image strategies

Pick the smallest base that works

Smaller base = smaller image = faster pull + smaller attack surface + fewer CVEs to track. The trade-off: less stuff to debug with.

Full distro

~300 MB

ubuntu, debian, fedora. Everything you'd expect on a real machine. Easy to debug; lots of CVEs to track.

Slim / Alpine

~5–80 MB

node:22-alpine, python:3.13-slim. Stripped-down distros. Most apps work; some musl/glibc gotchas with Alpine.

Distroless

~10–50 MB

gcr.io/distroless/nodejs22 etc. Just your app + runtime. No shell, no package manager, no debugger. Production-grade.

scratch

app size only

Literally empty. Only works for static binaries (Go, Rust). Image = just your binary. Tiny. Bulletproof.

Default to distroless for production runtime, slim/alpine for build stage, full distro only when you need debugging. Use scratch only for static binaries (Go, Rust, statically-linked C).

⏸ Pause and check

A multi-stage Dockerfile has:

Answer: b. Final image is 10–100× smaller because the runtime stage doesn't carry compilers, source code, or dev dependencies.

Section 2 · Before & After

Naive build vs multi-stage + distroless

The same Node.js app, two ways. The first is what you build the first time you write a Dockerfile. The second is what you build after one CVE scan.

Same Node app. Same functionality. 17× smaller. 24× fewer CVEs. No shell for attackers.
Before · 1.4 GB

One-stage Dockerfile. Started FROM ubuntu for "familiar" feel. Ran apt install for build tools. Copied source code. Ran npm install + build. Final image contains: full ubuntu, gcc, make, source code, npm cache, build artifacts, your app.

Slow to pull, slow to push, lots of CVEs to track. An attacker who breaks in has a full shell, package manager, and your source code.

After · 80 MB

Multi-stage Dockerfile. Stage 1 (builder): node:22, install deps, build app, produce /app/dist. Stage 2 (runtime): FROM gcr.io/distroless/nodejs22, copy only /app/dist from stage 1. Final image: distroless + your built artifact. No source code, no build tools, no shell.

17× smaller. Pulls in seconds. Far fewer CVEs. An attacker who breaks in has nothing — no shell to exec, no package manager to install tools.

What didn't change: the app's behaviour. Multi-stage + distroless is purely operational improvement — no code change. The cost: harder to debug a running container (no shell to exec into), so you build "debug variants" with shell when needed.

Section 3 · Analogy

The bakery

Building a container image is like running a bakery. You have a recipe (Dockerfile) pinned to the wall. You have an oven (BuildKit, Buildah, Kaniko — different brands of ovens, all bake the same kind of cake). You stack ingredients (base image + your code + dependencies) and out comes a layered cake (the OCI image).

The pros do three more things. They use a multi-stage process: a messy prep kitchen for chopping and mixing, a clean finishing kitchen that only handles the final cake. The customer never sees the messy prep kitchen. They use the smallest possible cake base — distroless or scratch — because customers don't want to eat the cardboard box. And they ship a complete ingredient list (SBOM) plus a baker's signature seal (image signature) so customers know what's in the cake and that it really came from the official bakery.

That's all "image building" is. Recipe → oven → cake. Add some discipline and your cake is small, audited, and signed.

In the story……in Kubernetes
The recipeThe Dockerfile (or Buildpacks descriptor, ko config, etc.)
The ovenThe image builder (BuildKit, Buildah, Kaniko, ko, Buildpacks)
Multi-stage bakingA multi-stage Dockerfile (build stage + runtime stage)
The cake baseThe base image (full distro vs alpine vs distroless vs scratch)
Ingredient list on the sideThe SBOM (SPDX or CycloneDX)
The baker's signature sealImage signing (cosign / sigstore)
Food safety inspectionVulnerability scanning (Trivy, Grype, Snyk)

⚠️ The analogy stops here: a real cake gets baked once and eaten. A container image gets pulled millions of times. Optimisation matters here in a way it doesn't for cakes.

Section 4 · Two-level explanation

Two ways to hear it

ELI5 · explain like I'm 5

A bakery. The recipe is a list of steps. The oven is the machine that bakes. You can use a small base (just a cookie) or a big base (a whole cake). Pros use the smallest base that works because nobody wants to eat the box. They also write down what's in the cake so you know what you're eating, and they sign their work so you know it came from them.

ELI10 · explain like I'm 10

An OCI image is built by feeding instructions (a Dockerfile) to a builder (BuildKit, Buildah, Kaniko, ko, or Buildpacks). Each instruction becomes a layer. The most important pattern is multi-stage: one stage with all the build tools and dev dependencies, a final stage that copies just the compiled artifact into a tiny base image. Common bases: full distro (300MB), alpine (~5MB), distroless (~10-50MB, no shell), scratch (literally empty, only works for static binaries). Then you scan for CVEs (Trivy/Grype), generate an SBOM (machine-readable ingredient list in SPDX or CycloneDX format), and sign the image (cosign). Together those three give you a defensible supply-chain story.

⏸ Pause and check

What's an SBOM?

Answer: b. When the next Log4Shell drops, an SBOM database lets you grep for the vulnerable library across hundreds of services in seconds. Without one, days.

Section 5 · Real-world scenarios

Where image-building decisions show up

Multi-stage cut image from 1.2 GB to 80 MB

A team's Node.js app shipped as a 1.2 GB image. Pulls took 90 seconds, deploys felt slow. They refactored to multi-stage: builder uses node:22 with all dev dependencies, final stage uses gcr.io/distroless/nodejs22 with only the built dist/ directory. Result: 80 MB image. Pulls take 6 seconds. CVE count dropped from 124 to 8. Same app behavior, 15× faster deploys.

Distroless prevented an attacker from getting a shell

A web app got compromised through an unsanitized input vulnerability. The attacker's payload tried to exec("/bin/sh") to escalate. The image used gcr.io/distroless/python3 — no shell included. The exec failed; the attacker had nothing to pivot to. The team fixed the input bug calmly during business hours. Distroless turned a "wake up at 3am" incident into a Tuesday afternoon ticket.

SBOM sped up the Log4j response

When the Log4Shell CVE landed in December 2021, every team scrambled to find which of their hundreds of services included the vulnerable Log4j version. Teams that had been generating SBOMs (SPDX/CycloneDX) for every image had a one-line answer: grep their SBOM database for "log4j-core". Teams without SBOMs spent days inspecting Dockerfiles and rebuilding images to check. SBOMs paid for themselves in a single incident.

Image signing caught a supply-chain attack

An attacker compromised a developer's CI credentials and pushed a malicious image to the team's registry under a legitimate tag. Production clusters had a Kyverno admission policy requiring images to be signed by the team's Cosign key. The malicious image wasn't signed (the attacker had registry creds but not the Cosign key). Kubernetes refused to admit the Pod. Incident contained at the admission boundary, not after the malicious code ran.

Section 6 · Animation

Watch a multi-stage build run

Three modes. The first builds a multi-stage image. The second compares image sizes by base. The third runs a CVE scan + SBOM + signature.

Watching: a multi-stage build. Stage 1 builds (big), stage 2 packages just the artifact (small).
Section 7 · Misconceptions, flashcards & quiz

Lock it in

Three common misconceptions to clear up first, then the flashcards and quiz.

Common Misconceptions

Myth: Distroless means "no Linux."
Truth: Distroless images include just enough Linux to run your app — and nothing else (no shell, no package manager, no debugger). The runtime is still Linux underneath.
Myth: Smaller image = always better.
Truth: Distroless and scratch are harder to debug. The right answer is "smallest base that runs your app and lets you investigate when needed" — sometimes that's a slim variant, not scratch.
Myth: Image signing prevents bugs.
Truth: Signing prevents tampering. It tells you the image was built by your pipeline and hasn't been swapped. It says nothing about whether the code is correct.
What is a Dockerfile?
tap to flip
A text file with build instructions (FROM, COPY, RUN, etc.) that a builder turns into an OCI image. Each instruction usually becomes a cached layer. Most popular image-build format; not the only one.
What is multi-stage build?
tap to flip
A Dockerfile pattern with multiple FROM stages. Build stage has compilers + dev deps (large). Runtime stage copies only the final artifact into a tiny base. Final image is 10-100× smaller than single-stage.
What is distroless?
tap to flip
Base images from gcr.io/distroless that contain only your app + minimal runtime — no shell, no package manager, no debugger. ~10-50 MB. Tiny attack surface; harder to debug a running container.
What is scratch?
tap to flip
An empty base image — literally nothing. Only works for static binaries (Go, Rust, statically-linked C). Image = just your binary. Bulletproof but no debugging.
Buildah vs Kaniko vs ko?
tap to flip
Buildah = daemonless OCI builder (Red Hat). Kaniko = builds inside an unprivileged container, perfect for K8s CI. ko = builds Go images without a Dockerfile, just point at the Go module.
What is multi-arch with buildx?
tap to flip
docker buildx --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 builds one image tag containing manifests for both architectures. Each node pulls the right one. Built using QEMU emulation under the hood.
What is an SBOM?
tap to flip
Software Bill of Materials. A machine-readable list of every component inside an image (libraries, OS packages, language deps, versions). Standard formats: SPDX, CycloneDX. Critical for CVE response (e.g., Log4Shell).
What is image signing?
tap to flip
Cryptographically prove the image was built by you and hasn't been tampered with. Tools: cosign (Sigstore project, easiest), Notary v2. Admission controllers (Kyverno, OPA) verify signatures before allowing the image to run.

Your Node.js app image is 1.2 GB and CVE scans flag 124 vulnerabilities (mostly in libraries you don't use). Production deploys feel slow. What's the highest-leverage change?

answerRefactor to multi-stage Dockerfile + distroless. Stage 1: FROM node:22 with all dev dependencies, runs npm ci + build. Stage 2: FROM gcr.io/distroless/nodejs22, copies /app/dist from stage 1. Likely result: ~80 MB image (15× smaller), ~5-10 CVEs (90% fewer because no extra OS packages). Deploys speed up dramatically. No app code change required.

A CVE drops in a popular library (like the next Log4Shell). Your team manages 80 services. How do you find which images include the vulnerable library?

answerQuery your SBOM database. If you generate SBOMs (SPDX or CycloneDX) for every image at build time and store them centrally (e.g., in Dependency-Track or your registry's SBOM endpoint), you grep for the library name in seconds. Teams without SBOMs spend days manually inspecting Dockerfiles and rebuilding images to check. Generate SBOMs in CI; you'll thank yourself the day a critical CVE lands.
🎬 Choose Your Own Adventure

An attacker compromises your CI credentials and pushes a malicious image to your registry under a legitimate tag. Your prod has a Kyverno admission policy requiring images to be signed by your team's Cosign key. Click to see what happens. ▼

at admissionThe malicious image isn't signed — the attacker had registry creds but not the Cosign key. Kubernetes refuses to admit the Pod. The incident is contained at the admission boundary, before any malicious code ran. Your incident retrospective is about why the CI creds were compromised, not about a breached cluster. Lesson: image signing turns a tag-swap from a breach into a denied admission.
Words from this lesson · open if you want a quick reference
Dockerfile
Text file with build instructions. Most popular image-build format. Each instruction becomes a layer.
BuildKit
Modern Docker builder. Default behind docker build. Parallelism, layer caching, secret mounts.
Buildah / Kaniko / ko
Alternative builders. Buildah = daemonless. Kaniko = unprivileged in-container. ko = Go-specific, no Dockerfile.
Cloud Native Buildpacks
Auto-detect language and build images without a Dockerfile. Used by Heroku, Cloud Foundry, Cloud Run.
Multi-stage
Dockerfile pattern with multiple FROM stages. Build with full toolchain, copy only artifact to small final stage.
Distroless
Google base images at gcr.io/distroless containing only app + runtime. No shell, no package manager.
SBOM
Software Bill of Materials. Machine-readable list of every component in an image. Formats: SPDX, CycloneDX.
cosign · Sigstore
Image signing tool from the Sigstore project (CNCF). Cryptographically sign and verify container images.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: Build small (multi-stage), ship smaller (distroless), sign your work, generate an SBOM, scan for CVEs. That's a production-grade image.

✓ Lesson 10 complete

Build small, multi-stage, distroless. Sign your work. Generate SBOMs. Scan for CVEs. That's a production-grade image.

Next — Lesson 11: Container Security & Registries. Rootless containers, read-only filesystems, registry auth + pull policies, the security default toolkit for production.

Suvis Guru · K-COM · Lesson 10 of 17 · grounded in docs.docker.com · github.com/GoogleContainerTools/distroless · sigstore.dev
Lesson 11· Container Security & Registries

📍 Today's stop in K-Town: K-Town Bank Vault Quarter.

📍 Bank Vault Quarter · lesson 11 of 16Lesson 11 of 16, Bank Vault Quarter.

Module 2 · Lesson 11 · how containers stay safe

Container Security · Rootless · Read-Only · Registries

Two halves: where the image came from (registry + auth) and how it runs (non-root, read-only, no extra powers). A bank vault, a teller, and a glass inspection booth.

🚨 The 3 AM Nightmare

Your web app gets compromised through an unsanitised input. The attacker gets a shell as root inside the container. They write a webshell to disk, escalate via a kernel CVE, pivot to the host, then to other Pods. By morning, your cluster is gone. The same bug, on a hardened Pod, is a Tuesday afternoon ticket. This lesson is the 12 lines of YAML that change which one happens.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: Where the image came from (auth + pull policy) plus how it runs (non-root, read-only, drop caps, seccomp) shrinks blast radius 10×.

Section 1 · Concept

Two halves: where it came from, how it runs

A container image alone is not secure. The runtime configuration around it decides whether a single bug becomes a breached cluster or a contained ticket. There are two halves to this story.

Half one — where the image came from. Public images on Docker Hub pull anonymously. Private images need credentials, supplied via an imagePullSecret. The kubelet uses that secret to authenticate to your private registry (ECR, GCR, ACR, Harbor, etc.). Without the secret, you get ImagePullBackOff. The imagePullPolicy then decides whether the kubelet re-pulls every start (Always — right for mutable tags), uses the cache if present (IfNotPresent — default for tagged images), or refuses to pull and uses only what's already on the node (Never).

Half two — how it runs. The Pod's securityContext decides what the container can do. runAsNonRoot refuses to run as UID 0. runAsUser pins a specific non-root UID. capabilities.drop: [ALL] strips Linux superpowers; add only what you need. readOnlyRootFilesystem makes the container's disk immutable. allowPrivilegeEscalation: false blocks setuid binaries from regaining privilege. seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault filters dangerous syscalls. Apply Pod Security Standards (Privileged / Baseline / Restricted) per namespace and let the API server enforce these defaults at admission, instead of relying on every developer to remember them.

The two halves compose. A signed image (Lesson 10) pulled with credentials (this lesson) running with a hardened securityContext (this lesson) is the production-grade story. Skip any half and you've left a door open.

Section 1.5 · The 5 building blocks, broken down

Each topic, one tile

👤

1 · Rootless / non-root

don't run as UID 0

Default in containers: root inside. One kernel bug → host root. Set runAsNonRoot: true + runAsUser: 1000.

🔒

2 · Read-only filesystem

no writes to disk

readOnlyRootFilesystem: true — attacker can't drop binaries. Mount emptyDir for paths that need writes (logs, /tmp).

⚡

3 · Capabilities + seccomp

drop ALL, add only needed

Linux superpowers cut from ~40 to 1. seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault blocks dangerous syscalls. Cheap, big payoff.

🗝️

4 · Registry auth

imagePullSecrets

Private registry needs creds. Create a docker-registry secret, reference via imagePullSecrets. Without it: ImagePullBackOff.

⏱️

5 · Pull policies

Always · IfNotPresent · Never

Always: re-pulls every start (use for :latest). IfNotPresent: cache OK (default for tags). Never: must already be local.

All five compound: non-root + read-only + drop-caps + private-registry + pinned-tag = production-grade Pod.

Section 1.7 · The securityContext spec

Six fields. Six locks.

The six fields you'll set on every production Pod, what each one means, and what it prevents.

runAsNonRoot
true
Refuse to start if image's USER is root.
Prevents host-root escape via kernel bug.
runAsUser
1000
Pin a specific non-root UID for the process.
Predictable file ownership; auditable identity.
allowPrivilegeEscalation
false
Block setuid binaries from regaining privilege.
setuid → root re-entry path.
readOnlyRootFilesystem
true
Make the container's rootfs immutable at runtime.
Attacker dropping malware on disk; tampering with binaries.
capabilities.drop
[ALL]
Strip all Linux superpowers. Add back only what's needed.
CAP_SYS_ADMIN, CAP_NET_ADMIN, CAP_PTRACE, etc. — ~40 dangerous primitives.
seccompProfile.type
RuntimeDefault
Use the runtime's default syscall filter.
Roughly 40–65 dangerous syscalls (kexec, ptrace modes, etc.; varies by runtime version).

Together these six match the Pod Security Standards "Restricted" profile. About 12 lines of YAML, massive blast-radius reduction.

Section 1.8 · Pull policies

When does the kubelet phone the registry?

Three options. The default depends on your tag — and that subtlety has caused more outages than people realize.

Always

What: Kubelet checks the registry on every Pod start. If the digest changed, re-pull.

Default for :latest and tags without an explicit version. Right when you genuinely want the freshest image.

IfNotPresent

What: Use the local cached image if any image with that name+tag is on the node. Otherwise, pull.

Default for tagged images (:v2, :1.4.7). Right for pinned, immutable versions. Be careful: re-pushing a tag does NOT re-pull.

Never

What: Don't contact the registry at all. The image must already be on the node.

Air-gapped clusters. Local development with kind load or minikube image load. Bare-metal disconnected sites.

Best practice for production: pin to a digest (image: my-app@sha256:...). Digests are immutable, so the pull-policy question evaporates.

Section 1.9 · Pod Security Standards

Three tiers Kubernetes ships out of the box

Apply per namespace via labels. The API server's PodSecurity admission controller enforces the tier — no third-party admission needed for these defaults.

Privileged

No restrictions

Anything goes. hostNetwork, privileged: true, root user — all allowed. The "I trust this workload completely" tier.

Use for: system Pods (CNI, CSI, node-exporter, kube-proxy). Almost never for app workloads.

Baseline

Block known-bad

No hostNetwork, no hostPID, no hostPath, no privileged: true, no dangerous capabilities. Root user still allowed.

Use for: legacy app namespaces being modernized. The minimal tier any production namespace should be at.

Restricted

Production-grade

Baseline + runAsNonRoot + capabilities.drop: [ALL] + allowPrivilegeEscalation: false + seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault.

Use for: every greenfield app namespace. The default for new clusters.

Apply with kubectl label namespace prod Pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted. Use warn and audit levels first to find existing violations before flipping to enforce.

⏸ Pause and check

Your image is in a private registry. Your Pod can't pull it (ImagePullBackOff). What's missing?

Answer: b. The kubelet needs credentials to pull from private registries. Create a kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson secret and reference it in the Pod spec via imagePullSecrets.

Section 2 · Before & After

Naive Pod vs hardened Pod

Same container. Same workload. Five settings change. The blast radius of a bug shrinks 10×.

Same workload. Five settings change. The same SSRF that takes out the cluster on the left becomes a Tuesday afternoon ticket on the right.
Before · wide open

No securityContext. Container runs as root with all 40 capabilities and a writable root filesystem. Image is my-app:latest from Docker Hub — anonymous pull, mutable tag. Default pullPolicy is Always for :latest — every restart hits the registry, sometimes pulling slightly different content.

An SSRF in the app gives an attacker a root shell. They write a webshell to /var/www, escalate to host via a kernel CVE, and pivot to the cluster.

After · locked down

Six-field securityContext: runAsNonRoot, runAsUser: 1000, drop: [ALL], readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault. Image pinned to digest from a private registry, pulled with an imagePullSecret. Namespace labeled Pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted.

The same SSRF lands in a process that's UID 1000, can't write to disk, can't escalate, can't make dangerous syscalls. Pod gets killed; deploy gets hot-fixed.

What didn't change: the app's behaviour. Hardening is operational, not functional. The cost is 12 lines of YAML and a Dockerfile USER 1000 directive.

Section 3 · Analogy

The fortified bank

A container registry is like a bank vault. Images are the safety deposit boxes; the vault door (registry auth) decides who gets to take one out. The teller window is the kubelet asking the registry, "I'm node-3, here are my credentials, give me image X." Without credentials, the teller turns you away — that's ImagePullBackOff.

Once the box is out, the visitor doesn't get to roam the bank. They go to a glass inspection booth — that's the container runtime. The booth has rules: non-root visitor (runAsNonRoot), no tools allowed (drop: [ALL] capabilities), the display case is sealed glass (readOnlyRootFilesystem), and a security camera overhead records every move (seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault). The bank's overall security manager sets which rules apply to which booths — that's Pod Security Standards.

In the story……in Kubernetes
The bank vaultThe container registry (Docker Hub, GHCR, ECR, GCR, ACR, Harbor)
The vault doorRegistry authentication
The teller windowThe kubelet pulling on behalf of the Pod
The customer's credentialsimagePullSecrets
The glass inspection boothThe container runtime sandbox
The visitor wristbandNon-root user (runAsUser: 1000)
The "no tools allowed" signcapabilities.drop: [ALL] + allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
The sealed glass display casereadOnlyRootFilesystem: true
The security cameraThe seccomp profile (RuntimeDefault)
The bank's security managerPod Security Standards (Privileged / Baseline / Restricted)

⚠️ The analogy stops here: a bank vault has one combination. A container has dozens of independent locks (each securityContext field). Skipping any one is leaving a door cracked.

Section 4 · Two-level explanation

Two ways to hear it

ELI5 · explain like I'm 5

Imagine a bank. The vault holds boxes — those are the images. You can't just walk in. The teller checks your card before handing one over. Once you have a box, you go to a glass room with a camera. You can't bring tools, can't break the glass, and you're not the manager. The bank decides what you can do.

ELI10 · explain like I'm 10

Container security has two layers. First, where did the image come from? You set imagePullSecrets so the kubelet can authenticate to your private registry, and you set imagePullPolicy so it knows when to re-fetch (Always for mutable tags, IfNotPresent for pinned versions). Second, how does it run? The Pod's securityContext decides: runAsNonRoot, runAsUser: 1000, capabilities.drop: [ALL], readOnlyRootFilesystem, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault. All together, this matches the Pod Security Standards "Restricted" profile. A bug in your app can no longer become a host-level compromise.

⏸ Pause and check

Pick the safest of these settings for a production Pod:

Answer: b. Each of those settings shrinks the blast radius of a bug. Together they match the Pod Security Standards "Restricted" profile.

Section 5 · Real-world scenarios

Where security defaults pay off

Container escape blocked by readOnly + non-root

A SaaS company's web app had an SSRF leading to RCE. The attacker landed inside the container as UID 1000 with a read-only filesystem and ALL capabilities dropped. They couldn't write a malicious binary, couldn't escalate to root, couldn't pivot to the host. The Pod was deleted; the deploy was hot-fixed by lunch. Same bug class in 2018 took out their staging cluster for a week.

Private registry pull failed silently — fixed with imagePullSecret

A team migrated images from Docker Hub to their company's private Harbor registry. Pods went into ImagePullBackOff. The kubelet logs said "401 Unauthorized." Fix: create a kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson secret with Harbor credentials and add imagePullSecrets: [name: harbor-creds] to the Pod spec — or better, attach the secret to the namespace's default ServiceAccount so every Pod inherits it. Pods rolled out cleanly.

Pull policy bug: re-pushing a tag silently kept old code running

A team used image: my-app:v2 and imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent. They re-pushed v2 with a hotfix instead of cutting v2.1. Most nodes still had the old v2 cached and never re-pulled — Pods kept running the old binary on those nodes and the new one on freshly-scaled nodes. Lesson: never re-push a tag. Cut a fresh version, or pin to @sha256:digest.

Pod Security Standards "Restricted" caught a privileged Pod attempt

A new dev tried to deploy a Pod with securityContext: { privileged: true } for "easier debugging." The namespace was labeled Pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted. The API server rejected the Pod at admission with a clear message: "violates PodSecurity 'restricted:latest' — privileged is not allowed." Conversation moved from "let's just allow it" to "let's fix the actual debugging tooling." Defaults won.

Section 6 · Animation

Watch admission, pull, and policy in motion

Three modes. The first runs a Pod through admission with security checks. The second pulls from a private registry. The third compares pull policies side-by-side.

Watching: a Pod manifest with hardened securityContext arriving at the API server.
Section 7 · Misconceptions, flashcards & quiz

Lock it in

Three common misconceptions to clear up first, then the flashcards and quiz.

Common Misconceptions

Myth: Running as root inside a container is safe because the container is isolated.
Truth: Container root + a kernel exploit = host root. Run as a non-root UID by default.
Myth: imagePullPolicy: Always is overkill.
Truth: For mutable tags it's the only safe policy. For digest-pinned images, the question is moot — same digest, same content, every time.
Myth: Pod Security Standards are something you have to install.
Truth: They're built into the Kubernetes API server (since 1.25). Apply via namespace labels — no third-party admission needed.
What does runAsNonRoot: true do?
tap to flip
Tells the kubelet to refuse the Pod if the image's USER is root (UID 0). Forces non-root execution. Pair with runAsUser: 1000 to pin a UID. The image's Dockerfile must include a USER directive that's non-root.
What does readOnlyRootFilesystem: true do?
tap to flip
Makes the container's root filesystem immutable at runtime. An attacker can't drop binaries or tamper with code. Mount an emptyDir at any path that needs writes (logs, /tmp).
Linux capabilities — best practice in a Pod?
tap to flip
drop: ["ALL"], then add only the specific capabilities your app needs (e.g., NET_BIND_SERVICE for ports under 1024). Default of "inherit all root powers" is dangerous.
What is an imagePullSecret?
tap to flip
A K8s secret of type kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson holding registry credentials. Referenced from a Pod's imagePullSecrets list (or attached to a ServiceAccount). Without it, kubelet can't pull from private registries — Pod stuck in ImagePullBackOff.
Three imagePullPolicy values?
tap to flip
Always — re-checks registry every start (right for :latest). IfNotPresent — use cache if present (default for tagged images). Never — must already be on the node (air-gapped, dev).
What is seccompProfile RuntimeDefault?
tap to flip
A built-in syscall filter that blocks roughly 40–65 dangerous syscalls (kexec, ptrace certain modes, etc.; varies by runtime version) while allowing what normal apps need. Cheap, effective hardening default.
Three Pod Security Standards levels?
tap to flip
Privileged (no restrictions). Baseline (block known-bad: hostNetwork, hostPath, privileged). Restricted (production-grade: non-root, drop caps, no privilege escalation, seccomp). Apply per namespace via labels.
Why pin images to digest (@sha256:...)?
tap to flip
Tags are mutable — someone can re-push :v2 with different content. Digests are immutable — @sha256:abc always means the exact same image. Pinning defeats supply-chain tag swaps and removes pull-policy guesswork.

You inherit a deployment with no securityContext. The image runs as root and writes a few log files into /var/log/myapp. What are the three highest-leverage hardening changes?

answer(1) runAsNonRoot: true + runAsUser: 1000 — prevents root inside the container, which is one host-kernel-bug away from root on the node. (2) capabilities.drop: [ALL] + allowPrivilegeEscalation: false — strips the ~40 Linux superpowers and prevents setuid binaries from regaining them. (3) readOnlyRootFilesystem: true with an emptyDir mounted at /var/log/myapp so the app can still write logs — prevents an attacker from dropping malware on disk. Add seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault for free syscall hardening. The image's Dockerfile may need a USER 1000 directive added.

Your Pod is stuck in ImagePullBackOff. The image is at private-registry.company.com/team/api:v3.2. You can pull it from your laptop with docker login. Why does the Pod fail and what's the fix?

answerYour laptop has a local docker config with credentials; the cluster does not. The kubelet can't authenticate to your private registry. Fix: create a Kubernetes secret of type kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson with kubectl create secret docker-registry registry-creds --docker-server=… --docker-username=… --docker-password=…, then reference it from the Pod via imagePullSecrets: [{ name: registry-creds }]. For convenience, attach the secret to the default ServiceAccount in that namespace so every Pod inherits it automatically.
🎬 Choose Your Own Adventure

A new dev tries to deploy a Pod with securityContext: { privileged: true } "for easier debugging." Your namespace is labeled Pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted. Click to see what happens. ▼

at admissionThe API server rejects the Pod at admission with a clear message: violates PodSecurity 'restricted:latest' — privileged is not allowed. The conversation moves from "let's just allow it" to "let's fix the actual debugging tooling." Defaults won. Lesson: opt-in security is opt-out reality. Set the namespace policy and let the API server hold the line.
Words from this lesson · open if you want a quick reference
securityContext
Pod/container spec block defining security behavior: user, capabilities, filesystem mode, seccomp profile, privilege escalation.
runAsNonRoot
Refuses to start a container whose effective UID is 0. Image must have a non-root USER directive.
readOnlyRootFilesystem
Mounts the container's rootfs read-only. Block attacker writes; mount emptyDir for paths that need writes.
capabilities
Linux feature splitting root power into ~40 pieces. Best practice: drop: [ALL], add only what's needed.
seccomp
Linux syscall filter. Pod field seccompProfile.type: RuntimeDefault uses the runtime's curated default — blocks roughly 40–65 dangerous syscalls (varies by runtime version).
imagePullSecret
K8s secret of type kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson holding registry credentials. Referenced by Pod or ServiceAccount.
imagePullPolicy
Pod field controlling when the kubelet contacts the registry: Always, IfNotPresent, Never.
Pod Security Standards
Built-in K8s admission tiers: Privileged (anything), Baseline (block known-bad), Restricted (production-grade). Apply per namespace.
ImagePullBackOff
Pod status meaning the kubelet failed to pull the image and is backing off retries. Almost always: missing/wrong imagePullSecret or wrong tag.
image digest
Immutable SHA-256 of the image manifest (@sha256:abc...). Pinning to digest defeats tag mutation.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: Where the image came from (auth + pull policy) plus how it runs (non-root, read-only, drop caps, seccomp) shrinks blast radius 10×.

✓ Lesson 11 complete

Two halves: where the image came from (auth + pull policy), and how it runs (non-root, read-only, drop caps, seccomp). Six lines of securityContext shrinks blast radius 10×.

Next — Lesson 12: PID 1 & Container Lifecycle. Why PID 1 inside a container matters, signal handling, graceful shutdown, zombies and orphans, init systems (tini, dumb-init), preStop hooks.

Suvis Guru · K-COM · Lesson 11 of 17 · grounded in kubernetes.io/docs · Pod-security-standards · imagePullSecrets
Lesson 12· PID 1 & Container Lifecycle

📍 Today's stop in K-Town: K-Town Harbour.

📍 K-Town Harbour · lesson 12 of 16Lesson 12 of 16, K-Town Harbour.

Module 2 · Lesson 12 · how containers shut down

PID 1 · Signals · Graceful Shutdown · Init Wrappers

A Pod doesn't just die — it follows a four-step shutdown protocol. The captain (PID 1) hears the lighthouse (SIGTERM), tells the crew (child processes), waits the grace period, then lights out.

🚨 The 3 AM Nightmare

It's 3 AM. Your deploy went out clean. But somehow 4% of customer orders vanished mid-checkout, and your logs just say Killed. By morning you have 47 angry support tickets and no idea which Pods got hit. This lesson is about why that happens — and the one line of YAML (ENTRYPOINT ["tini", "--", ...]) that prevents it.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: PID 1 inside your container has two jobs Linux normally hands to systemd — forward signals and reap zombies. Wrap your entrypoint in tini. Handle SIGTERM. Add a preStop sleep. Tune the grace period.

Section 1 · Concept

The shutdown protocol nobody told you about

When a Pod terminates, the kubelet doesn't just yank the plug. It runs a four-step protocol that depends on whoever is running as process 1 inside your container doing the right thing. Most "my Pod takes 30 seconds to die" mysteries trace back to PID 1.

Why PID 1 is special. Linux gives PID 1 (the first process started in a namespace) two responsibilities the kernel won't take care of for anyone else: (1) forward signals to its descendants when it gets them, and (2) reap zombies — call waitpid() on every child that exits, so the kernel can free the process table entry. On a normal Linux host, systemd does this. Inside a container, your ENTRYPOINT process is PID 1 — and most apps don't know they're supposed to do those jobs.

Signals K8s sends. When a Pod is deleted, the kubelet sends SIGTERM to PID 1 — the polite "please wrap up." If PID 1 hasn't exited within terminationGracePeriodSeconds (default 30s), the kubelet sends SIGKILL — the kernel-level "die now," uncatchable.

Graceful shutdown. A well-behaved app catches SIGTERM, stops accepting new work, finishes in-flight requests, flushes buffers, closes connections, exits 0. Pod status moves Running → Terminating → Terminated.

Init wrappers. tini and dumb-init are tiny binaries that solve both PID-1 problems. ENTRYPOINT ["tini", "--", "your-app"]: tini becomes PID 1, your app is PID 2. tini forwards every signal to PID 2 and reaps any orphan zombie that gets re-parented to it. One line, two big problems gone.

preStop hook. A Pod-level escape hatch that runs before SIGTERM is sent. Common pattern: preStop: sleep 10 — gives the load-balancer time to notice the Pod is being removed before connections stop. Critical for zero-downtime rolling updates.

Section 1.5 · The 5 building blocks, broken down

Each topic, one tile

👑

1 · PID 1 problem

first process gets two jobs

PID 1 must forward signals to children and reap zombies. The kernel won't do those for anyone else.

📨

2 · Signals

SIGTERM → SIGKILL

K8s sends SIGTERM first, then SIGKILL after grace. SIGKILL is uncatchable. SIGINT is local-only.

⏱️

3 · Graceful shutdown

drain, flush, exit 0

App catches SIGTERM, stops accepting work, finishes in-flight, flushes, exits 0. Within terminationGracePeriodSeconds.

👻

4 · Zombies & orphans

parent must reap

Exited child stays as a zombie until parent calls waitpid(). Orphans get re-parented to PID 1 — which must reap them too.

🛡️

5 · Init wrappers

tini · dumb-init

ENTRYPOINT ["tini", "--", "app"]. tini forwards signals + reaps zombies. Two PID-1 problems gone in one line.

All five compound: tini PID 1 + SIGTERM handler + preStop drain + tuned grace = clean, lossless shutdown.

Section 1.7 · Pod lifecycle phases

The five phases a Pod walks through

The Pod's status.phase is a high-level summary. Container-level state (Waiting, Running, Terminated) lives in status.containerStatuses[].

Pending → Running → Succeeded / Failed / Unknown
Pending: scheduled but not yet running (image pulling, init containers running).
Running: at least one container started; Pod is bound to a node.
Succeeded: all containers exited 0; restartPolicy will not restart.
Failed: at least one container exited non-zero; restartPolicy will not restart.
Unknown: kubelet can't reach the node — usually a network partition.

Container state changes more frequently than Pod phase: a Running Pod can have a Waiting container that's restarting after a crash.

Section 1.8 · Lifecycle hooks

postStart and preStop

Two hook points. Both run inside the container. Both can be exec or HTTP. Use them sparingly — most apps shouldn't need either.

postStart

Fires after the container is created, asynchronously to the entrypoint. No guarantee it runs before the entrypoint.

Use for: registering with a service mesh, warming a cache. Avoid: anything the app actually depends on (use init containers instead).

preStop

Fires before the kubelet sends SIGTERM. Blocks SIGTERM until preStop finishes (or grace period expires).

Use for: sleep 10 to let the load-balancer drain, kill -SIGUSR1 to trigger a graceful flush, draining Kafka consumers.

preStop is the most underused trick in K8s. A 10-second sleep here eliminates the LB-drain race in most rolling updates.

Section 1.9 · terminationGracePeriodSeconds

How long does the kubelet wait?

Wall-clock seconds between "preStop completes / SIGTERM sent" and SIGKILL. Default is 30. Tune it.

30s · default

Right for stateless web apps that drain quickly. Too short for: databases, queue workers in the middle of a long task, big buffer flushes.

120–600s · tuned

For databases (let WAL flush), batch workers (let current task finish), apps with large in-memory buffers. terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 300 is common for stateful workloads.

If the kubelet is impatient (e.g., during node drain), you can also be SIGKILL'd before grace expires. Don't rely on grace being honored under all conditions — make shutdown idempotent.

⏸ Pause and check

Your container's Dockerfile has CMD ["bash", "start.sh"] and start.sh runs node server.js &. K8s sends SIGTERM. What happens?

Answer: b. Bash is PID 1 and doesn't forward signals to background children. Node never learns the Pod is shutting down. After 30s grace, SIGKILL. In-flight requests dropped.

Section 2 · Before & After

Naive shutdown vs proper shutdown

Same Node app. Same signal arrives. Two different shutdowns — one drops requests, one doesn't.

Same SIGTERM arrives. The naive shutdown wastes 30 seconds and drops requests; the proper one finishes in under 8 seconds with zero loss.
Before · bash as PID 1

Dockerfile: CMD ["bash", "/app/start.sh"]. start.sh runs node server.js & in the background. SIGTERM arrives → bash receives it but doesn't forward to Node → Node never gets the signal → 30 seconds elapse → SIGKILL → Node dies mid-request, in-flight requests drop, no flush. Logs show "Killed". Operators wonder why every deploy is "lossy."

After · tini + preStop

Dockerfile: ENTRYPOINT ["/usr/bin/tini", "--"] + CMD ["node", "server.js"]. Pod adds lifecycle.preStop.exec.command: ["sh","-c","sleep 5"] and terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 60. preStop runs first, then tini forwards SIGTERM to Node, Node closes Express server gracefully, exits 0. Zero requests dropped.

What didn't change: the app code (assuming Node already handles SIGTERM, which Express does with one line). What changed: PID 1 actually delivers the signal.

Section 3 · Analogy

The ship's captain

A container shutdown is a ship docking and disembarking. The captain is PID 1. The crew (child processes) are at their stations: engine room, cargo hold, navigation. Out at sea, the lighthouse flashes a signal — that's SIGTERM from the kubelet — meaning "wrap up and head to port."

A good captain hears the signal, picks up the megaphone, and forwards the order to every crew member: "stop loading, wrap up the manifest, prepare to disembark." A bad captain hears the lighthouse and just stands there. The crew never gets the message and keeps loading cargo.

The captain also keeps a roster log — every crew member who disembarks gets ticked off (zombie reaping). Crew members whose original supervisor has already left get re-assigned to the captain (orphans), and the captain ticks them off when they leave too.

After the lighthouse signals, the harbour gives the ship a grace period (terminationGracePeriodSeconds, default 30s) to disembark peacefully. If the ship is still moored after grace, the harbour pulls the gangway and lights out (SIGKILL — can't be ignored). A captain who doesn't act in time loses crew mid-disembarkation.

In the story……in Kubernetes
The captain on the bridgePID 1 (your ENTRYPOINT process)
The crew at stationsChild processes
The lighthouse signalSIGTERM (the polite shutdown signal)
The captain's megaphoneSignal forwarding (PID 1 re-shouting SIGTERM to children)
The roster logZombie reaping (waitpid on each finished child)
Crew re-assigned to PID 1Orphan processes adopted by PID 1
The grace-period clockterminationGracePeriodSeconds
Lights outSIGKILL (uncatchable, kernel-level)
The pre-disembark announcementThe preStop hook
Hiring a professional captainWrapping the entrypoint in tini / dumb-init

⚠️ The analogy stops here: a real captain can refuse to leave port. PID 1 cannot refuse SIGKILL — it's uncatchable. The only way to leave on your own terms is to leave during the grace period.

Section 4 · Two-level explanation

Two ways to hear it

ELI5 · explain like I'm 5

A captain on a ship. The lighthouse blinks "time to leave." The captain picks up the megaphone and tells everyone. The crew packs up. The captain checks every name on a list as they leave. Then lights out. If the captain ignores the lighthouse, the harbour just turns out the lights anyway and people get stuck mid-task.

ELI10 · explain like I'm 10

Inside a container, the first process you start is PID 1 and gets two special jobs Linux normally hands to systemd: forward signals to descendants, and reap zombie children. When K8s wants to delete a Pod, it runs the optional preStop hook, then sends SIGTERM to PID 1. PID 1 forwards that to every child and waits for them to exit. If PID 1 doesn't exit within terminationGracePeriodSeconds (default 30), K8s sends SIGKILL — uncatchable. If PID 1 is bash, it swallows SIGTERM and your app never learns the Pod is shutting down. Wrap your entrypoint in tini (ENTRYPOINT ["tini", "--", "node", "server.js"]) — it forwards signals + reaps zombies for free. Add a preStop: sleep 5 if you need the load-balancer to drain.

⏸ Pause and check

During rolling updates, a small fraction of requests return 502 for a few seconds. Pods are healthy. The app handles SIGTERM correctly. What's the cause?

Answer: b. The endpoints update is slower than the app's shutdown. Fix: add a preStop hook that sleeps 10s before SIGTERM is sent — gives the LB time to drain the Pod from rotation.

Section 5 · Real-world scenarios

Where PID 1 quietly wrecks things

Bash-as-PID-1 dropped SIGTERM — every deploy was lossy

A team's deploys consistently took the full 30 seconds and ended with logs showing "Killed". Investigation: Dockerfile used CMD bash startup.sh and startup.sh ran node server.js & in the background. Bash was PID 1, caught SIGTERM, didn't forward it. Node never knew. Fix: ENTRYPOINT ["tini", "--"] + CMD ["node", "server.js"]. Deploys became instant and graceful.

30-second grace was too short — DB shutdown got truncated

A team's PostgreSQL sidecar was getting SIGKILL'd mid-checkpoint during Pod shutdowns. Result: occasional WAL corruption requiring recovery. Fix: terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 300 for that Pod, plus a preStop hook running pg_ctl stop -m smart. The DB gets up to 5 minutes to flush cleanly. Corruption stopped.

preStop sleep saved zero-downtime rolling updates

During rolling updates, a fraction of requests returned 502. Cause: Pod was shutting down before the Service's endpoint controller had updated the load-balancer; the LB kept sending traffic to a closed port. Fix: preStop: { exec: { command: ["sh", "-c", "sleep 10"] } }. Pod sits idle for 10s after being marked for deletion, giving the LB time to remove it from rotation. 502s dropped to zero.

Zombie process accumulation crashed a Java app under load

A Java app spawned subprocesses for thumbnail generation. Each finished quickly, but the JVM didn't reap them. Zombies accumulated until the process table filled, then "Resource temporarily unavailable" errors. Pod restart cleared it temporarily. Real fix: ENTRYPOINT ["tini", "--", "java", "-jar", "app.jar"]. tini reaped zombies as they appeared. Crashes stopped.

Section 6 · Animation

Watch shutdown, signal forwarding, and reaping

Three modes. Graceful shutdown timeline, bad-vs-good PID 1 signal forwarding, and zombie reaping in action.

Watching: the four-step shutdown protocol. preStop → SIGTERM → drain → exit.
Section 7 · Misconceptions, flashcards & quiz

Lock it in

Three common misconceptions to clear up first, then the flashcards and quiz.

Common Misconceptions

Myth: SIGKILL is "just a stronger SIGTERM."
Truth: SIGKILL is uncatchable, kernel-level. Your app cannot clean up under SIGKILL — it just dies. That's why graceful shutdown matters.
Myth: terminationGracePeriodSeconds is a hint.
Truth: It's a hard wall-clock timer. SIGKILL fires at the end. Tune it (60s, 300s) for slow shutdowns — databases, queue workers, big flushes.
Myth: Zombies are theoretical.
Truth: A Java app spawning subprocesses without waitpid() will fill the PID table over hours. Real outage. tini solves it for free.
Why is PID 1 special inside a container?
tap to flip
Linux gives PID 1 two jobs the kernel won't do for anyone else: forward signals to descendants, and reap zombie children (waitpid). On a host, systemd does this. In a container, your ENTRYPOINT process is PID 1.
SIGTERM vs SIGKILL — when does K8s send each?
tap to flip
On Pod deletion, kubelet sends SIGTERM to PID 1 (polite "wrap up"). If PID 1 hasn't exited within terminationGracePeriodSeconds (default 30s), kubelet sends SIGKILL — uncatchable.
What does tini do?
tap to flip
Tiny init binary. ENTRYPOINT ["tini", "--", "your-app"]. tini becomes PID 1, app is PID 2. tini forwards every signal to PID 2 and reaps any zombie re-parented to it. Solves both PID-1 problems.
What is a zombie process?
tap to flip
A process that has exited but whose entry remains in the process table because the parent hasn't called waitpid(). Zombies don't use CPU or RAM but consume PID slots. Accumulating zombies fill the table.
What is preStop and what's it for?
tap to flip
Pod hook that runs BEFORE SIGTERM is sent. Common pattern: preStop sleep 10 — gives the load-balancer time to notice the Pod is being removed before connections stop. Saves rolling updates from 502s.
terminationGracePeriodSeconds — what is it?
tap to flip
Seconds the kubelet waits between sending SIGTERM and SIGKILL. Default 30. Tune higher (120-600+) for slow shutdowns: databases, queue workers, big flushes.
Why is CMD ["bash", "start.sh"] dangerous as entrypoint?
tap to flip
bash becomes PID 1, catches SIGTERM but doesn't forward it to spawned children. App never learns the Pod is shutting down → SIGKILL → dropped requests. Use tini or exec the app directly.
Five Pod phases?
tap to flip
Pending (scheduled, not running yet), Running (at least one container started), Succeeded (all containers exited 0), Failed (at least one container exited non-zero), Unknown (kubelet can't reach node).

Your team's deploys consistently take exactly 30 seconds and end with "Killed" in logs. Dockerfile has CMD ["bash", "/app/start.sh"] and start.sh runs node server.js &. Why slow and lossy, and what's the fix?

answerbash is PID 1 inside the container. SIGTERM arrives, bash receives it, but bash does NOT forward signals to its background children. Node never learns the Pod is shutting down. Kubelet waits the full 30s grace, then SIGKILL — uncatchable, drops in-flight requests. Two fixes: (1) Use tini as PID 1: ENTRYPOINT ["tini", "--"], CMD ["node", "server.js"]. tini forwards SIGTERM to Node and reaps zombies. (2) Or skip the wrapper: ENTRYPOINT ["node", "server.js"] — Node becomes PID 1 and gets SIGTERM directly. Make sure Node has a SIGTERM handler that closes the HTTP server gracefully.

During rolling updates, a small fraction of requests return 502s for a few seconds. Pods are healthy. The app handles SIGTERM correctly and shuts down in under a second. Cause and fix?

answerRace condition between Pod termination and load-balancer endpoint removal. When K8s marks a Pod for deletion, two things start in parallel: kubelet sends SIGTERM (and your app shuts down), AND the endpoints controller updates the Service to remove the Pod from the LB. The endpoints update is slower than your shutdown. So briefly the LB is still sending traffic to a Pod whose port has already closed → 502. Fix: add a preStop hook that sleeps for ~10 seconds before the app receives SIGTERM. lifecycle: { preStop: { exec: { command: ["sh", "-c", "sleep 10"] } } }. The sleep blocks SIGTERM until the LB has had time to drain the Pod from rotation.
🎬 Choose Your Own Adventure

You hire a rookie captain (bash) for your container ship. The lighthouse blinks SIGTERM. The captain… does nothing. He didn't think it applied to him. Click to see what happens to the crew. ▼

30 seconds later
  harbour clock:  0s ───────────── 30s ───── 💀
  crew status:    [loading]    [still loading]   [SIGKILL]
  passengers:     ████████████████████ 47% lost
The harbour waited the full 30-second grace, then pulled the gangway with SIGKILL. The crew never got the message and were still loading cargo when the lights went out. Fix: hire tini as your captain — he forwards every signal he receives, on time, every time. ENTRYPOINT ["tini", "--", "node", "server.js"]. One line.
Words from this lesson · open if you want a quick reference
PID 1
First process in a Linux namespace. Must forward signals to descendants and reap zombie children. In a container, your ENTRYPOINT process.
SIGTERM
Polite "please exit" signal (number 15). What K8s sends first when terminating a Pod. Catchable, ignorable.
SIGKILL
Forced "die now" signal (number 9). Sent after grace period expires. Kernel-level — cannot be caught or ignored.
terminationGracePeriodSeconds
Pod field. Wall-clock seconds the kubelet waits between SIGTERM and SIGKILL. Default 30. Tune higher for slow shutdowns.
preStop hook
Pod lifecycle hook that runs BEFORE SIGTERM. Common pattern: sleep 10 to let the LB drain.
postStart hook
Pod hook that runs after the container is created, asynchronously to the entrypoint. Use sparingly.
tini · dumb-init
Tiny init binaries that run as PID 1 inside containers. Forward signals to your app and reap zombies. Often pre-installed in language images.
zombie process
Exited process whose entry remains in the process table until parent calls waitpid(). Doesn't use CPU/RAM but consumes a PID slot.
orphan process
Child process whose parent has exited. Re-parented to PID 1, which becomes responsible for reaping it on exit.
Pod phase
High-level Pod state: Pending, Running, Succeeded, Failed, Unknown. Container states (Waiting, Running, Terminated) are tracked separately.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: PID 1 inside your container has two jobs Linux normally hands to systemd — forward signals and reap zombies. Wrap your entrypoint in tini. Handle SIGTERM. Add a preStop sleep. Tune the grace period.

✓ Lesson 12 complete · Module 2 done

PID 1 forwards signals and reaps zombies. Wrap your entrypoint in tini, handle SIGTERM, add preStop for LB drain, tune the grace period. Container Module 2 wraps here.

Next — Module 3: Kubernetes Architecture & API. Lesson 13 — Cluster Architecture: control plane, nodes, etcd, the API server, controller manager, scheduler.

Suvis Guru · K-COM · Lesson 12 of 17 · grounded in kubernetes.io/docs · github.com/krallin/tini · github.com/Yelp/dumb-init
Lesson 13· Cluster Architecture

📍 Today's stop in K-Town: K-Town International Airport.

📍 K-Town International Airport · lesson 13 of 16Lesson 13 of 16, K-Town International Airport.

Module 3 · Lesson 13 · how a cluster is built

Cluster Architecture · Control Plane & Nodes

A cluster is two halves: a control tower (control plane — decides) and several terminals (worker nodes — execute). One radio between them: the API server.

🚨 The 3 AM Nightmare

A network partition isolates 2 of your 3 etcd nodes from each other. Quorum is lost. The cluster goes read-only — no new Pods scheduled, no failover, no Deployments updated. Anything currently running keeps running, but the cluster is frozen in amber. You can't even apply the fix. This lesson is about the brain–muscle split that makes Kubernetes work, and where it breaks if you're not careful.

🎯 If you remember nothing else: Control plane decides; nodes execute. Five control-plane components, three node components, one API door between them. Memorise the 6-hop Pod-creation flow.

Section 1 · Concept

Two halves: brain and muscle

A Kubernetes cluster is two distinct halves: a control plane (the brain — decides what should run) and one or more worker nodes (the muscle — actually runs the containers). They are different machines with different jobs, talking over a network.

The control plane. Five processes, usually on dedicated control-plane nodes (and never running your app workloads):

• API server (kube-apiserver) — the only door into the cluster. Every read, write, watch, authentication request goes here. Stateless, horizontally scalable.
• etcd — strongly-consistent key-value store holding the entire cluster state. Source of truth.
• Controller manager (kube-controller-manager) — runs dozens of reconcile loops (Deployment, ReplicaSet, Node, Endpoints, ServiceAccount, Job…). Each watches the API for desired state, compares to actual, takes action.
• Scheduler (kube-scheduler) — picks which node runs each newly-created Pod, based on resource requests, taints, affinity, topology.
• Cloud controller manager (cloud-controller-manager) — bridges K8s to the cloud (load balancers, persistent disks, node lifecycle). Optional on bare metal.

The worker nodes. Every node — control plane or worker — runs three things:

• kubelet — the node agent. Watches the API server for Pods assigned to its node, tells the runtime to start/stop them, reports node status.
• kube-proxy — manages the local network rules (iptables, IPVS, eBPF) that implement Services on this node.
• Container runtime — containerd, CRI-O, or runc-based — actually starts and stops the containers (Lesson 09).

The constraint. Nothing talks directly to etcd. Nothing talks directly between controllers. All communication goes through the API server. That single-door discipline is the architectural constraint that makes the rest of K8s possible: scaling, watching, RBAC, audit, admission — every mechanism is built on top of "everything is a typed object you read/write through the API server."

Section 1.5 · The 5 control-plane components

Each one, one tile

📡

1 · API server

the only door

REST + watch endpoints. Validates, authenticates, persists to etcd, fans out watches. Stateless · scales horizontally.

🗄️

2 · etcd

source of truth

Strongly-consistent KV store. Holds every K8s object. Odd-member quorum (3 or 5). Lose majority → cluster read-only.

🔄

3 · Controller manager

reconcile loops

Bundle of loops: Deployment, ReplicaSet, Node, Endpoints, Job, etc. Each watches API for desired vs actual, takes action.

🎯

4 · Scheduler

picks the node

For each unscheduled Pod: filter feasible nodes, score them, pick highest. Resource fit · taints · affinity · topology.

☁️

5 · Cloud controller mgr

bridge to provider

Translates K8s objects into AWS/GCP/Azure API calls (LBs, disks, node info). Optional on bare metal.

All five compound: API + etcd + controllers + scheduler + cloud-cm = the brain that survives any single failure.

Section 1.7 · Worker node components

Three things on every node

The control plane decides; these three actually do the work on each node.

🛠️

kubelet

Node agent. Watches API server for Pods bound to this node, asks the runtime to start them, reports status (CPU, memory, disk, conditions) back. Also runs liveness/readiness probes.

🚦

kube-proxy

Implements Kubernetes Services on this node — programs iptables, IPVS, or eBPF rules so traffic to a Service's virtual IP gets load-balanced across the right Pod IPs.

🐳

container runtime

containerd, CRI-O, or runc-based. Pulls images, manages container lifecycle, sets up namespaces/cgroups. Speaks the CRI (Container Runtime Interface) to the kubelet.

Together they form the "node runtime" — the per-machine subsystem that turns control-plane decisions into actually running containers.

Section 1.8 · How a Pod gets created

The end-to-end flow, in 6 hops

This is the request path that every Kubernetes object follows. Memorize it; it explains 90% of the questions you'll have about K8s behavior.

1kubectl→API serverPOST /api/v1/namespaces/default/Pods (your YAML)
2API server→etcdValidate, authorize, run admission, persist Pod object
3scheduler→API serverWatches new Pods · picks node-3 · POSTs Binding
4kubelet (n3)→API serverWatches Pods bound to node-3 · sees the new Pod
5kubelet→runtimeCRI: pull image, create container, start container
6kubelet→API serverPATCH Pod status: phase=Running, containerStatuses

Notice every step routes through the API server. The scheduler never tells the kubelet anything directly — it writes a Binding, the kubelet's watch picks it up. That's the design.

Section 1.9 · HA control plane

What "production-ready" means

One control-plane node is fine for learning. Production needs replication. Two pieces matter: API server replication and etcd quorum.

API server: 3+ replicas behind a LB

API server is stateless — etcd holds all state. Run 3 (or 5) instances behind a load-balancer. Any can serve any request. Lose one, the LB routes around it. Controller manager and scheduler also run with leader election: only one of the three is active at a time, others stand by; if the leader dies, a new one wins the lease in etcd within ~15s.

etcd: odd-member quorum

[ deep dive — skip if new ]etcd uses Raft. Writes need a majority (quorum). 3 members tolerate 1 failure; 5 members tolerate 2. Always odd (4 members tolerate the same as 3 — 1 — and you've added latency). Co-locate one etcd per CP node, or run them as a separate cluster (stacked vs external topology). Leader-election internals (term numbers, vote requests, log replication) live in the Raft paper if you want them; for operating Kubernetes, "lose majority → read-only" is the load-bearing fact.

Lose quorum (e.g., 2 of 3 etcd dead) and the cluster goes read-only — gets work, no writes, no failover, no scheduling. This is why etcd backups and quorum-zone planning matter.

Section 1.10 · Managed vs self-managed

Who runs the control plane?

Managed (EKS · GKE · AKS · DO · Linode)

Cloud provider runs the control plane for you. You manage worker nodes (or use managed node pools too). Pay ~$73/cluster/mo (GKE / EKS) or sometimes free (AKS, GKE Autopilot). Patching, etcd backups, HA — all theirs. Best for teams that aren't full-time cluster operators.

Self-managed (kubeadm · kops · k3s · RKE)

kubeadm sets up a vanilla CP on your VMs. k3s is a tiny K8s for edge/IoT. RKE automates the install. You own everything: etcd, certs, HA, upgrades. More control, more ops time. Bare-metal, edge, regulated environments, or 100+ clusters where managed-per-cluster cost adds up.

For most teams under 50 engineers: managed. The control plane is undifferentiated heavy lifting and the hourly rate is silly cheap compared to engineering time spent on it.

⏸ Pause and check

Which component is the only door into a Kubernetes cluster?

Answer: b. Every read, every write, every watch goes through the API server. Nothing talks to etcd directly. That's the design.

Section 2 · Before & After

Single-machine app vs cluster-native app

The same workload, before and after the control-plane / nodes split.

Left: one process is your scheduler, your state store, and your app — and your single point of failure. Right: those concerns are factored apart, and any node can disappear without the cluster noticing for more than a few seconds.
Before · single machine

One process running your app + a "scheduler" + a "config store" all together. Process dies → everything dies. Adding a second machine? Now they need to coordinate state — and you've invented half of K8s, badly.

After · CP + nodes

Control plane is one logical unit (HA-replicated underneath). Worker nodes are interchangeable cattle. The Pod you deployed yesterday survives a node failure tonight, because the control plane noticed and recreated it elsewhere.

What changed: separation of concerns. The control plane decides; the nodes do. Every K8s feature builds on top of this split.

Section 3 · Analogy

The airport: tower + terminals

A Kubernetes cluster is an airport with one control tower and several terminal buildings. The tower (the control plane) is where all the decisions happen. The terminals (worker nodes) is where aircraft (your Pods) actually park, board, and fly.

Inside the tower, five rooms: radio dispatch at the top window (API server — every plane radios here, nobody else has authority to broadcast); flight plan archive in the basement (etcd — every plane that ever existed, every assignment, written down); specialist controllers (controller manager — "always-3-planes-on-this-route", "this-plane-needs-a-gate", "node-just-went-dark"); runway assignment desk (scheduler — picks which terminal/runway each plane goes to); and a cloud liaison (cloud-controller-manager — radio link to outside power, fuel, navigation aids).

Inside each terminal, three roles: gate manager (kubelet — receives assignments, executes them, reports back); ground crew router (kube-proxy — directs taxiing planes via the local taxiway map); tug operator (container runtime — actually moves the planes).

The tower never directly grabs a plane. It writes the assignment to the archive; the gate manager picks it up. The gate manager never calls another terminal directly; it goes through the tower's radio. That single-door discipline is what lets the airport scale to hundreds of terminals.

In the story……in Kubernetes
The control towerThe control plane (the brain)
Radio dispatchThe API server (the only door)
The flight-plan archiveetcd (source of truth)
The specialist controllerskube-controller-manager (reconciliation loops)
The runway-assignment deskkube-scheduler
The cloud liaisoncloud-controller-manager
A terminal buildingA worker node
The gate managerThe kubelet
The ground-crew routerkube-proxy
The aircraft at the gateA Pod

⚠️ The analogy stops here: an airport tower has one radio operator. The Kubernetes API server is replicated 3+ ways behind a load balancer — multiple operators, all serving the same view, any can fail.

Section 4 · Two-level explanation

Two ways to hear it

ELI5 · explain like I'm 5

A control tower talks to terminals. Terminals park planes. The tower decides where each plane goes, the terminal makes it happen. If a plane crashes, the tower puts up a new one somewhere else. If a terminal closes, the tower moves all its planes to another terminal.

ELI10 · explain like I'm 10

A K8s cluster has a brain (control plane) and muscles (worker nodes). The brain has 5 parts: an API server (the only door in), etcd (where state is stored), a controller manager (lots of small loops keeping desired state matching reality), a scheduler (picks which node runs which Pod), and a cloud-controller-manager (bridges to AWS/GCP/Azure). Each worker node runs kubelet (the node agent that runs your Pods), kube-proxy (network rules for Services), and a container runtime (containerd or CRI-O). When you kubectl apply, your YAML goes to the API server, which writes it to etcd; the scheduler picks a node and writes the binding back; the kubelet on that node reads its assignment and tells the runtime to start the container. Everything talks through the API server, nothing talks directly.

⏸ Pause and check

Your 3-member etcd cluster loses 2 nodes. What happens?

Answer: b. 3 members tolerate 1 failure. Lose 2, you're below quorum. 5-member etcd tolerates 2 failures.

Section 5 · Real-world scenarios

Where architecture decisions show up in production

etcd lost quorum during a network partition — cluster went read-only

A 3-member etcd cluster across three AZs hit a network partition that isolated 2 of 3 nodes from each other. Quorum couldn't be achieved → etcd switched to read-only → API server could serve gets but not writes → no new Pods scheduled, no failover, no Deployments updated. Lesson: 5-member etcd survives 2 failures; 3-member only survives 1. Cross-AZ etcd needs careful quorum-zone planning.

API server overloaded by an aggressive controller

A custom controller was watching all Pods and re-LISTing every second. At 8000 Pods, that hammered the API server with hundreds of MB/s of JSON. API server fell behind on writes, kubectl timed out cluster-wide. Fix: use proper informers (LIST once + WATCH for diffs) and respect server-side rate limits. Standard controller-runtime libraries do this for you.

Managed CP (GKE) saved a startup from etcd backup nightmares

A 12-engineer startup ran self-managed K8s on EC2 with kubeadm. They spent ~30 hours/month on etcd backups, version upgrades, and CP patching. Migrated to GKE. Engineering time on cluster ops dropped to zero. They paid $73/month per cluster; they paid one engineer ~$14k/month before. Math is what it is.

Pricing as of 2024: GKE Standard ~$73/cluster/mo; AKS free; EKS $73/mo; GKE Autopilot bills per-Pod. Check your provider's current pricing — the conclusion (managed beats self-managed at this team size) doesn't change.

kube-scheduler bug pinned all Pods to one node

A scheduler bug in a specific minor version assigned all newly-created Pods to the same node, ignoring resource constraints. That node OOM'd and got cordoned. Subsequent Pods went to the next node, also OOM'd. Cascading failure. Workaround: deploy a second scheduler (multi-scheduler is supported via schedulerName on the Pod spec) until the upstream fix landed. Real fix: upgrade.

Section 6 · Animation

Watch the cluster work

Three modes. Pod creation end-to-end, a controller reconcile loop in action, and an HA control plane surviving a node failure.

Watching: kubectl → API → etcd → scheduler → kubelet → runtime → Running.
Section 7 · Misconceptions, flashcards & quiz

Lock it in

Three common misconceptions to clear up first, then the flashcards and quiz.

Common Misconceptions

Myth: The control plane runs your apps.
Truth: Control plane decides; worker nodes run. In production they're usually different machines, often with taints to keep workloads off the CP.
Myth: Components talk directly to each other.
Truth: Everything goes through the API server. The scheduler doesn't tell the kubelet anything — it writes a Binding; the kubelet's watch picks it up.
Myth: Self-managed Kubernetes is "more flexible."
Truth: For most teams under 50 engineers, managed (EKS / GKE / AKS) is a no-brainer. Self-managed wins for specific cases: bare metal, regulated environments, or 100+ clusters.
Five control-plane components?
tap to flip
API server (only door in), etcd (state store), controller manager (reconcile loops), scheduler (picks nodes), cloud-controller-manager (bridges to cloud APIs).
What does the API server do?
tap to flip
The only door into the cluster. Validates, authenticates, persists to etcd, fans out watches. Stateless and horizontally scalable. Every other component talks to it.
Why is etcd special?
tap to flip
Strongly-consistent KV store holding the entire cluster state. Source of truth. Uses Raft consensus; needs odd-member quorum (3 or 5). Lose majority → cluster goes read-only.
What does the controller manager do?
tap to flip
Runs dozens of reconcile loops as a single binary: Deployment, ReplicaSet, Node, Endpoints, Job, ServiceAccount, etc. Each watches API for desired state, compares to actual, takes action.
What does the scheduler do?
tap to flip
Picks which node runs each newly-created Pod. Considers resource requests, taints/tolerations, affinity, topology spread. Writes the binding back to the API server.
Three components on every worker node?
tap to flip
kubelet (node agent — runs assigned Pods, reports status), kube-proxy (programs iptables/IPVS/eBPF for Services), container runtime (containerd/CRI-O — actually starts containers).
Trace 'kubectl apply Pod.yaml'.
tap to flip
kubectl → API server (validates) → etcd (persisted) → scheduler (picks node) → API server (writes binding) → kubelet on that node (sees its Pod) → runtime (pulls image, starts) → Pod Running. Status flows back via the API server.
Managed vs self-managed control plane?
tap to flip
Managed (EKS/GKE/AKS): cloud runs the CP, you manage worker nodes. ~$75/mo per cluster. Self-managed (kubeadm/kops/k3s/RKE): you run all of it. Most teams under 50 engineers choose managed.

You run kubectl apply -f Pod.yaml. Trace the request end-to-end: which components touch it, in what order, and what does each do?

answer(1) kubectl POSTs the YAML to the API server, authenticated with your kubeconfig. (2) The API server runs admission, validates, applies defaults, and writes the Pod object to etcd. (3) etcd persists across its quorum. The API server returns 201 Created. (4) The scheduler is watching the API server for unscheduled Pods. It evaluates fit, picks node-3, POSTs a Binding to the API server. (5) The kubelet on node-3 is watching the API server for Pods bound to itself. It sees the new Pod, reads the spec, and asks the container runtime (containerd) to pull the image and start the container. (6) The kubelet PATCHes Pod status (Running) back to the API server, which writes it to etcd. kubectl get Pod sees Running. Notice: nothing talks directly to etcd; nothing talks between scheduler and kubelet directly. The API server is the only door.

Your 3-member etcd is split across three AZs. A network partition isolates AZ-1 from AZ-2 and AZ-3 (but AZ-2 and AZ-3 still talk). What happens? How would 5 members have changed it?

answerAZ-2 and AZ-3 still have 2 of 3 etcd members → quorum (majority) is achieved → those 2 form the active etcd group, accept writes, cluster keeps running. AZ-1's etcd is isolated and goes read-only. So a 3-member etcd survives this partition fine, as long as 2 members remain together. But: if the partition isolated EACH AZ separately, no group has 2 members and the whole cluster goes read-only. With 5 members spread 1-2-2 across 3 AZs, two AZs together have quorum. Bottom line: 3-member tolerates 1 failure, 5-member tolerates 2, but topology of the partition matters as much as the count.
🎬 Choose Your Own Adventure

You run kubectl apply -f Pod.yaml. Click to trace exactly what happens. ▼

trace
  1. kubectl  → API server     (POST /api/v1/.../Pods)
  2. API srvr → etcd           (validate, persist)
  3. scheduler watches API     (picks node-3, POSTs Binding)
  4. kubelet on node-3 watches (sees its Pod)
  5. kubelet  → runtime        (pull image, start)
  6. kubelet  → API server     (PATCH status: Running)
Notice every hop routes through the API server. The scheduler never tells the kubelet anything directly. That single-door discipline is the architectural constraint that makes K8s scale.
Words from this lesson · open if you want a quick reference
Control plane
The "brain" of the cluster: API server, etcd, controller manager, scheduler, cloud-cm. Decides what should run.
API server
The only door into the cluster. REST + watch endpoints over HTTPS. Stateless; horizontally scalable. Every other component talks to it.
etcd
